author: Seth Godin
text: Our current system of teaching kids to sit in straight rows and obey instructions isn’t a coincidence—it was an investment in our economic future. The plan: trade short-term child-labor wages for longer-term productivity by giving kids a head start in doing what they’re told. Large-scale education was not developed to motivate kids or to create scholars. It was invented to churn out adults who worked well within the system.
narration: The way we teach kids to sit in straight rows and follow instructions isn't accidental; it was a deliberate investment in our economic future. The idea was to trade short-term child labor for long-term productivity by training kids to do what they're told. Large-scale education wasn't designed to inspire kids or create scholars; it was meant to produce adults who fit neatly into the system.

author: Seth Godin
text: As long as we embrace (or even accept) standardized testing, fear of science, little attempt at teaching leadership, and most of all, the bureaucratic imperative to turn education into a factory itself, we’re in big trouble.
narration: If we continue to accept standardized testing, fear of science, and the bureaucratic push to turn education into a factory, we're heading for serious trouble.

author: Seth Godin
text: School has become an industrialized system, working on a huge scale, that has significant byproducts, including the destruction of many of the attitudes and emotions we’d like to build our culture around.
narration: Education has turned into an industrialized system that operates on a massive scale, leading to significant byproducts, including the erosion of the attitudes and emotions we want to nurture in our culture.

author: Seth Godin
text: The economy needed an institution that would churn out compliant workers, so we built it. Factories didn’t happen because there were schools; schools happened because there were factories. The reason so many people grow up to look for a job is that the economy has needed people who would grow up to look for a job.
narration: The economy required an institution to produce compliant workers, which is why we created schools. Factories emerged not because schools existed, but because factories were already in place. This is why many people grow up seeking jobs; the economy has always needed individuals who are prepared to look for work.

author: Seth Godin
text: Learning is not done to you. Learning is something you choose to do.
narration: Learning isn't something that happens to you; it's a choice you make.

author: Seth Godin
text: The real shortage we face is dreams, and the wherewithal and the will to make them come true. No tweaks. A revolution.
narration: What we truly lack are dreams and the resources and determination to realize them. It's time for a revolution, not just minor adjustments.

author: Seth Godin
text: The shortcut to compliance, then, isn’t to reason with someone, to outline the options, and to sell a solution. No, the shortcut is to induce fear.
narration: The easy path to compliance isn't through reasoning or presenting options; it's through instilling fear.

author: Seth Godin
text: Instead of creating a social marketplace where people engage and grow, school is a maelstrom, a whirlpool that pushes for sameness and dumbs down the individual while it attempts to raise the average.
narration: Rather than fostering a social marketplace for engagement and growth, schools often become a chaotic force that promotes conformity and stifles individuality while trying to elevate the average.

author: Seth Godin
text: It turns out that industrializing the schooling of billions of people was a natural fit, a process that quickly turned into a virtuous cycle: obedient students were turned into obedient teachers, who were then able to create even more obedient students. The cornerstone of the entire process was how well the notion of obedience fit into the need for education. We needed educated workers, and teaching them to be obedient helped us educate them. And we needed obedient workers, and the work of educating them reinforced the desired behavior.
narration: Industrializing the schooling of billions was a natural fit, creating a cycle where obedient students became obedient teachers, who then produced even more obedient students. The foundation of this process was how well obedience aligned with the need for education. We needed educated workers, and teaching them obedience facilitated that education, while the need for obedient workers reinforced the behavior we sought.

author: Seth Godin
text: The obligation of the new school is to teach reasonable doubt.
narration: The new school must focus on teaching reasonable doubt.

author: Seth Godin
text: Every day, beginning the first day and continuing until the last day, our teachers and our administrators and yes, most parents, seeking to do the right thing, end up doing the wrong one. We mean well. We let our kids down easy. We tell ourselves that we are realistic. We demand that students have a trade to fall back on, an assembly-line job available just in case the silly dreams don’t come true. And then, fearing heartbreak, we push them to bury the dream and focus on just the job.
narration: From the first day to the last, teachers, administrators, and parents, all with good intentions, often end up doing the wrong thing. We let our kids down easy, convincing ourselves that we are being realistic. We push them to bury their dreams and focus on securing a job, fearing heartbreak.

author: Seth Godin
text: Education isn’t a problem until it serves as a buffer from the world and a refuge from the risk of failure.
narration: Education becomes problematic when it acts as a buffer from the world and a refuge from the risk of failure.

author: Seth Godin
text: The industrialized, scalable, testable solution is almost never the best way to generate exceptional learning. Teaching is no longer about delivering facts that are unavailable in any other format. We’re not only avoiding issues of practicality and projects and hands-on use of information; we’re also aggressively testing for trivia.
narration: The industrialized, scalable, and testable approach rarely leads to exceptional learning. Teaching has shifted away from merely delivering facts that can be found elsewhere. We're not just neglecting practical issues and hands-on learning; we're also focused on testing for trivial knowledge.

author: Seth Godin
text: Academics are a means to an end, not an end. School is still about obedience and compliance and consumption, but now, layered on top of it, are hours every day of brute-force learning about how the world actually works.
narration: Academics serve as a means to an end, not the end itself. School continues to focus on obedience, compliance, and consumption, while also imposing hours of intense learning about the world.

author: Seth Godin
text: We ought to be asking school to make something different.
narration: We should be demanding that schools create something different.

author: Seth Godin
text: The real debate if you’re a worker is: do you want a job where they’ll miss you if you’re gone, a job where only you can do it, a job where you get paid to bring yourself (your true self) to work? Because those jobs are available. School is clearly organized around the second race.
narration: The real question for workers is whether you want a job where you're truly missed, where your unique contributions matter, or a job where you're just another cog in the machine. Clearly, schools are designed for the latter.

author: Seth Godin
text: School is a factory, and the output of that factory is compliant workers who buy a lot of stuff. These students are trained to dream small dreams. If the mass-processing of students we call school were good at creating the dreamers we revere, there’d be far more of them. In fact, many of the famous ones, the successful ones, and the essential ones are part of our economy despite the processing they received, not because of it.
narration: School operates like a factory, producing compliant workers who consume. Students are conditioned to limit their dreams. If traditional schooling truly fostered the dreamers we admire, we would see many more of them. In reality, many successful individuals thrive despite their schooling, not because of it.

author: Seth Godin
text: School teaches us otherwise.
narration: School teaches us a different lesson.

author: Seth Godin
text: The myth says: Great performance in school leads to happiness and success. And the corollary: Great parents have kids who produce great performance in school. It doesn’t matter that neither of these is true. What matters is that finding a path that might be better is just too risky for someone who has only one chance to raise his kids properly.
narration: There's a prevalent myth that excelling in school guarantees happiness and success, and that great parents raise high-performing kids. The truth is, these beliefs are often false. Yet, for many parents, exploring alternative paths feels too risky when they have only one chance to raise their children.

author: Seth Godin
text: The industrial model of school is organized around exposing students to ever increasing amounts of stuff and then testing them on it. Collecting dots. Almost none of it is spent in teaching them the skills necessary to connect dots.
narration: The traditional school model focuses on inundating students with information and then testing their recall. It's all about collecting facts, with little emphasis on teaching them how to connect those facts meaningfully.

author: Seth Godin
text: If you’re running an institution based on compliance and obedience, you don’t reach for motivation as a tool. It feels soft, even liberal, to imagine that you have to sell people on making the effort to learn what’s on the agenda.
narration: In an institution built on compliance and obedience, motivation isn't prioritized. It seems too soft or liberal to think about encouraging students to engage with the learning material.

author: Seth Godin
text: What’s clear to me is that teaching first graders words like “cogent” and “retrograde” isn’t the point. It’s not important that a six-year-old know that. What is important, vitally important, is that her teacher believes she could know it, ought to know it, and is capable of knowing it. We’ve been spending a fortune in time and money trying to stop teachers from doing the one and only thing they ought to be doing: coaching.
narration: It's evident that teaching young children complex words isn't the main goal. What truly matters is that the teacher believes in the child's potential to learn and grow. We've wasted so much time and resources trying to prevent teachers from doing what they should be doing: coaching.

author: Seth Godin
text: Learning is frightening for many because at any step along the way, you might fail. You might fail to get the next concept, or you might fail the next test. Easier, then, to emotionally opt out, to phone it in, to show up because you have to, because then failure isn’t up to you; it’s the system’s fault.
narration: For many, learning can be intimidating because there's always the risk of failure. It's often easier to disengage, to just go through the motions, and blame the system for any shortcomings.

author: Seth Godin
text: The question worth asking is: why bother? One reason is that quadratic equations are the gateway to calculus, which is the gateway to higher math. Another reason is that many of the elements of Newtonian mechanics involve similar sorts of analysis. Both reasons are based on the notion that a civilized society learns as much as it can, and advancing math and science (and thus engineering) requires a wide base of students who are educated in this subject so that a few can go on to get advanced degrees.
narration: It's worth questioning the purpose of teaching quadratic equations. They serve as a gateway to calculus and higher math, which are essential for advancing in fields like engineering and science.

author: Seth Godin
text: If we can’t (or won’t, or don’t want to) win the race to the bottom, perhaps we could seriously invest in the race to the top.
narration: If we can't win the race to the bottom, maybe we should focus on investing in the race to the top.

author: Seth Godin
text: When we let our kids dream, encourage them to contribute, and push them to do work that matters, we open doors for them that will lead to places that are difficult for us to imagine. When we turn school into more than just a finishing school for a factory job, we enable a new generation to achieve things that we were ill-prepared for. Our job is obvious: we need to get out of the way, shine a light, and empower a new generation to teach itself and to go further and faster than any generation ever has.
narration: By allowing our kids to dream and encouraging them to contribute, we can open doors to unimaginable possibilities. If we transform school into something beyond just a preparation for factory jobs, we empower a new generation to achieve what we couldn't. Our role is clear: we must step aside, illuminate their paths, and enable them to teach themselves and surpass any previous generation.

author: Seth Godin
text: If school is worth the effort (and I think it is), then we must put the effort into developing attributes that matter and stop burning our resources in a futile attempt to create or reinforce mass compliance.
narration: If school truly holds value, we need to focus our efforts on nurturing meaningful attributes rather than wasting resources on enforcing conformity.

author: John Holt
text: Most children in school fail. For a great many, this failure is avowed and absolute. Close to forty percent of those who begin high school, drop out before they finish. For college, the figure is one in three. Many others fail in fact if not in name. They complete their schooling only because we have agreed to push them up through the grades and out of the schools, whether they know anything or not.
narration: Most children in school fail, and for many, this failure is clear and undeniable. Nearly forty percent of those who start high school drop out before finishing, and for college, the dropout rate is one in three. Many others fail in reality, even if not in name, completing their schooling only because we push them through the grades, regardless of their actual knowledge.

author: John Holt
text: They fail because they are afraid, bored, and confused. They are afraid, above all else, of failing, of disappointing or displeasing the many anxious adults around them, whose limitless hopes and expectations for them hang over their heads like a cloud. They are bored because the things they are given and told to do in school are so trivial, so dull, and make such limited and narrow demands on the wide spectrum of their intelligence, capabilities, and talents.
narration: Children fail because they feel afraid, bored, and confused. They fear disappointing the anxious adults around them, whose high hopes loom over them like a cloud. They feel bored because the tasks assigned in school are trivial and dull, making only limited demands on their vast intelligence and talents.

author: John Holt
text: The schools in which the experiences described here took place are private schools of the highest standards and reputation. With very few exceptions, the children whose work is described are well above the average in intelligence and are, to all outward appearances, successful, and on their way to 'good' secondary schools and colleges.
narration: The schools where these experiences occurred are private institutions known for their high standards. Most of the children described are above average in intelligence and seem successful, heading toward 'good' secondary schools and colleges.

author: John Holt
text: If a certain kind of teaching failed to produce learning the first time, why will it suddenly produce it the second time? In many cases the children, now ashamed and angry as well as bored and confused, will do even worse than before—and will probably disrupt the class as well.
narration: If a specific teaching method failed to foster learning the first time, why would it succeed the second time? Often, children who are now ashamed, angry, bored, and confused will perform even worse and may disrupt the class.

author: John Holt
text: For children, the central business of school is not learning, whatever this vague word means; it is getting these daily tasks done, or at least out of the way, with a minimum of effort and unpleasantness. Each task is an end in itself. The children don't care how they dispose of it. If they can get it out of the way by doing it, they will do it; if experience has taught them that this does not work very well, they will turn to other means, illegitimate means, that wholly defeat whatever purpose the task giver may have had in mind.
narration: For children, school isn't really about learning; it's about getting through daily tasks with as little effort and discomfort as possible. Each task feels like a goal in itself, and kids will do whatever it takes to get it done, even if it means resorting to dishonest methods.

author: John Holt
text: If we look at children only to see whether they are doing what we want or don't want them to do, we are likely to miss all the things about them that are the most interesting and important. This is one reason why so many classroom teachers, even after years of experience, understand so little about the real nature of children.
narration: When we focus solely on whether children are following rules, we risk overlooking their most fascinating qualities. This is why many teachers, despite their experience, often fail to grasp the true nature of their students.

author: John Holt
text: Only as teachers in schools free themselves from their traditional teacher tasks—boss, cop, judge—will they be able to learn enough about their students to see how best to be of use to them.
narration: Teachers need to break free from their traditional roles as authority figures to truly understand and support their students.

author: John Holt
text: The reason we so seldom ask the question is that we tend to assume that unless there's something wrong with the student, all teaching produces learning, so that all we need to think about is what children should be made to learn. Once we understand that some of the things we teachers do may be helpful, some merely useless, and some downright harmful, we can begin to ask which is which.
narration: We rarely ask ourselves which aspects of teaching actually help or hinder learning. We often assume that if a student isn't learning, it's their fault, not considering that our teaching methods might be ineffective or even harmful.

author: John Holt
text: What I did not see until later was that we, our classroom, our position as teachers, which is to say, givers of orders, judges, graders, were the source of these children's strategies. We, and not math, or reading, or spelling, or history, were the problem that the children had designed their strategies to cope with.
narration: I eventually realized that our roles as teachers—essentially as authority figures—were the root of the problem. It wasn't the subjects themselves that hindered learning, but our approach to teaching.

author: John Holt
text: This is the most important task of a teacher, certainly of younger children—to make or make accessible a part of the world or of human experience which is as interesting, exciting, meaningful, transparent, and emotionally safe as possible.
narration: The primary responsibility of a teacher, especially for younger students, is to create an environment that is engaging, meaningful, and emotionally safe.

author: John Holt
text: Practically everything we do in school tends to make children answer-centered. In the first place, right answers pay off. Schools are a kind of temple of worship for 'right answers,' and the way to get ahead is to lay plenty of them on the altar. Thus their ways of teaching children, and, above all, the sheer volume of work they give them, will force the children into answer-directed strategies, if only because there isn't time for anything else.
narration: Everything we do in school tends to make children focus on getting answers. Right answers are rewarded, and schools become temples where these answers are worshipped. This approach forces children into strategies aimed at finding answers quickly, leaving little time for deeper thinking.

author: John Holt
text: What I am saying is that they were already smart. What Gattegno did, for an hour or so, was to put within their reach a miniature universe on which they could exercise the intelligence they already had, a universe in which they could do real things and see for themselves whether what they had done worked or not.
narration: What I'm saying is that these children were already intelligent. Gattegno created a small universe where they could explore and use their intelligence, allowing them to engage with real tasks and discover for themselves what worked.

author: John Holt
text: The question I have been trying to answer for many years is: Why don't they learn what we teach them? The answer I have come to boils down to this: Because we teach them--that is, try to control the contents of their minds.
narration: I've been pondering a crucial question for years: why don't students learn what we teach? The answer I've arrived at is simple: it's because we try to control what goes into their minds.

author: John Holt
text: It begins to look as if the test-examination-marks business is a gigantic racket, the purpose of which is to enable students, teachers, and schools to take part in a joint pretense that the students know everything they are supposed to know, when in fact they know only a small part of it--if any at all.
narration: It seems like the whole testing and grading system is just a big facade, allowing students, teachers, and schools to pretend that students know everything they're supposed to, when in reality, they grasp only a fraction of it.

author: John Holt
text: What is this test nonsense, anyway? Do people go through life taking math tests, with other people telling them to hurry? Are we trying to turn out intelligent people, or test takers?
narration: What’s the point of all these tests? Do we really expect people to go through life taking math tests under pressure? Are we aiming to create intelligent individuals or just good test takers?

author: John Holt
text: But we'll never do it as long as we are obsessed with tests. For all our talk and good intentions, there is much more stick than carrot in school, and while this remains so, children are going to adopt a strategy aimed above all else at staying out of trouble. How can we foster a joyous, alert, wholehearted participation in life if we build all our schooling around the holiness of getting 'right answers'?
narration: We'll never achieve this as long as we're fixated on tests. With more punishment than reward in schools, children will focus on avoiding trouble. How can we encourage joyful and engaged participation in life if our education system revolves around the sanctity of getting the 'right answers'?

author: John Holt
text: What kind of an adult is she going to grow up to be? How is she ever going to be able to make any sense of the world she will have to live in? What kind of fortresses of delusion and false security is she going to build for herself in her mind? It is hard not to feel that in every way it would have been much better for her never to have had to study arithmetic at all.
narration: What kind of adult will she become? How will she make sense of the world she'll live in? What false securities will she create in her mind? It's hard not to think it would have been better for her never to have studied arithmetic at all.

author: John Holt
text: We teachers, from primary school through graduate school, all seem to be hard at work at the business of making it look as if our students know more than they really do. Our standing among other teachers, or of our school among other schools, depends on how much our students seem to know; not on how much they really know, or how effectively they can use what they know.
narration: From primary school to graduate school, we teachers seem focused on making it appear that our students know more than they actually do. Our reputation among peers and schools relies on how much our students seem to know, rather than on their actual understanding or ability to apply that knowledge.

author: John Holt
text: Not only does the examination racket do harm by making students feel that a search for honest understanding is beside the point; it does further harm by discouraging those few students who go on making that search in spite of everything. The student who will not be satisfied merely to know 'right answers' or recipes for getting them will not have an easy time in school, particularly since facts and recipes may be all that his teachers know.
narration: The examination system harms students by making them feel that seeking honest understanding is pointless. Those who refuse to settle for just knowing the 'right answers' struggle in school, especially when their teachers only know facts and recipes.

author: John Holt
text: Curiosity, questions, and speculation--these are for outside school, not inside. Boredom and resistance may cause as much stupidity in school as fear. Give a child the kind of task he gets in school, and whether he is afraid of it, or resists it, or is willing to do it but bored by it, he will do the task with only a small part of his attention, energy, and intelligence.
narration: Curiosity and questions thrive outside of school, while boredom and resistance contribute to students' struggles. When faced with school tasks, children often engage only a fraction of their attention and energy.

author: John Holt
text: To a very great degree, school is a place where children learn to be stupid. A dismal thought, but hard to escape. Infants are not stupid. Children of one, two, or even three throw the whole of themselves into everything they do. They embrace life, and devour it; it is why they learn so fast and are such good company.
narration: School often teaches children to be stupid, a dismal reality. Young children fully engage with life, learning quickly and joyfully, but that curiosity fades in the school environment.

author: John Holt
text: What an extraordinary view of life, from one of the favored citizens of this most favored of all nations! Is life nothing but drudgery, an endless list of dreary duties? Is education nothing but the process of getting children ready to do them?
narration: It's a shocking perspective to view life as mere drudgery, filled with endless duties. Is education really just about preparing children for that?

author: John Holt
text: We adults destroy most of the intellectual and creative capacity of children by the things we do to them or make them do. We destroy this capacity above all by making them afraid, afraid of not doing what other people want, of not pleasing, of making mistakes, of failing, of being wrong. Thus we make them afraid to gamble, afraid to experiment, afraid to try the difficult and the unknown.
narration: We adults often destroy the intellectual and creative potential of children through our actions and expectations. We instill fear in them—fear of not meeting others' expectations, fear of making mistakes, and fear of failure. This fear inhibits their willingness to take risks, experiment, and tackle the difficult and unknown.

author: John Holt
text: We encourage them to feel that the end and aim of all they do in school is nothing more than to get a good mark on a test, or to impress someone with what they seem to know. We kill, not only their curiosity, but their feeling that it is a good and admirable thing to be curious, so that by the age of ten most of them will not ask questions, and will show a good deal of scorn for the few who do.
narration: We push children to believe that the ultimate goal of their schoolwork is merely to achieve good grades or impress others with their knowledge. This mindset stifles their curiosity and leads many to stop asking questions by the age of ten, often looking down on those who still do.

author: John Holt
text: We think it our right and our duty, not to tell the truth, but to say whatever will best serve our cause--in this case, the cause of making children grow up into the kind of people we want them to be, thinking whatever we want them to think.
narration: We often believe it is our duty to shape children's thoughts and beliefs, prioritizing our agenda over the truth. This manipulation serves to mold them into the kind of individuals we desire, rather than allowing them to form their own perspectives.

author: John Holt
text: We cannot have real learning in school if we think it is our duty and our right to tell children what they must learn. We cannot know, at any moment, what particular bit of knowledge or understanding a child needs most, will most strengthen and best fit his model of reality. Only he can do this.
narration: We can't achieve genuine learning in schools if we believe it's our responsibility to dictate what children must learn. At any given moment, we can't truly know what specific knowledge or understanding a child needs most to enhance their understanding of the world. Only the child can determine that.

author: John Holt
text: The alternative--I can see no other--is to have schools and classrooms in which each child in his own way can satisfy his curiosity, develop his abilities and talents, pursue his interests, and from the adults and older children around him get a glimpse of the great variety and richness of life.
narration: The only alternative I see is to create schools and classrooms where each child can explore their curiosity, nurture their talents, and pursue their interests, while also gaining insights from the adults and older children around them about the diverse and rich experiences life has to offer.

author: John Holt
text: You know, kids really like to learn; we just don't like being pushed around.
narration: Kids genuinely enjoy learning; they just don't appreciate being pushed around.

author: John Holt
text: I was struck by the difference between the 10s (whom I liked very much) and the 1s and 2s. The children in the classroom, despite their rich backgrounds and high I.Q.'s, were with few exceptions frightened, timid, evasive, and self-protecting. The infants at home were bold adventurers. It soon became clear to me that children are by nature and from birth very curious about the world around them, and very energetic, resourceful, and competent in exploring it, finding out about it, and mastering it.
narration: I noticed a stark contrast between the ten-year-olds in my classroom and the infants at home. The classroom children, despite their advantages, were often frightened and timid, while the babies were bold adventurers. It became clear to me that children are naturally curious and capable explorers of the world.

author: John Holt
text: I had come to feel strongly that the kinds of changes I wanted to see in schools, above all in the ways teachers related to students, could not happen as long as schools were compulsory. Since compulsory school attendance laws force teachers to do police work and so prevent them from doing real teaching, it would be in their best interests, as well as those of parents and children, to have those laws repealed, or at least greatly modified.
narration: I strongly believed that the changes I envisioned in schools, especially in teacher-student relationships, couldn't occur under compulsory schooling. These laws force teachers into roles akin to police, hindering genuine teaching. Repealing or modifying these laws would benefit everyone involved.

author: John Holt
text: Most of what I knew, I had not learned in school, or in any other such school like 'learning environments' or 'learning experiences'.
narration: I realized that most of what I knew didn't come from school or any formal learning environments.

author: John Holt
text: These parents do not want their children in or anywhere near classes in which children learn what interests them most, for the satisfaction and joy of doing it. They want their children to believe what countless teachers and parents have told me: 'If I wasn't made to do things, I wouldn't do anything.'
narration: These parents often resist the idea of their children engaging in classes that foster genuine interest and joy in learning. They instill the belief that without coercion, children wouldn't take initiative.

author: John Holt
text: One reason people take their children out of school is that they think they aren't learning anything. In an article in the March 1978 issue of the Radcliffe Quarterly I pointed out that, with few exceptions, schools are appallingly incompetent at their work, even as they define it, having found it easier to blame all their failures on their students.
narration: Many parents choose to take their children out of school because they feel their kids aren't learning anything. As I noted in a 1978 article, schools often fail at their core mission, preferring to blame students for these shortcomings.

author: John Holt
text: Though the courts have not yet agreed, compulsory school attendance laws, in and of themselves, seem to me a very serious infringement of the civil liberties of children and their parents, and would be so no matter what schools were like, how they were organized, or how they treated children.
narration: While courts may not see it this way, I believe that compulsory school attendance laws fundamentally infringe on the civil liberties of both children and their parents, regardless of how schools operate.

author: John Holt
text: It is nobody's proper business that a certain child got a certain mark in a certain course when she or he was eight years old.
narration: It's simply not anyone's business what grades a child received at such a young age.

author: John Holt
text: I have come not to feel obligated to rear and teach my children after any pattern or program devised, pre-scribed or presented by someone else or in such a manner as to meet any 'standards' set for them by any arbitrary political system. My children are well mannered, respectful to their parents, to their elders, to their peers, and to one another, are interested in life in general; they ask many questions which serve as the basis for their instruction one by one, day by day, and they appear articulate beyond their peers without their own family.
narration: I've realized that I don't have to raise my children according to any prescribed patterns or standards set by arbitrary political systems. My children are well-mannered, respectful, and curious about life, asking questions that guide their learning every day.

author: John Holt
text: Most people, however, take their children out of schools not so much for philosophical or political reasons as for the more direct and personal reason expressed by the Muslim mother: to prevent the schools from hurting their children, or hurting them any more than they already have.
narration: Many parents withdraw their children from school not just for philosophical reasons, but to protect them from the harm that schools can inflict.

author: John Holt
text: Many parents have written that their children's physical health improved strikingly once they were out of school. The changes that have occurred in Ishmael since we took him out of school have been unbelievable. Gone are the fits of temper that erupted every day around 4 P. M., gone are the headaches, the lines of tension around his mouth, and gone is his depression.
narration: Many parents report significant improvements in their children's health after leaving school. For instance, Ishmael's transformation has been remarkable; his temper fits, headaches, and depression have all disappeared.

author: John Holt
text: Putting children into a fixed or six-hour program at school is just squashing them. All the spirit and spunk they have when they are little is just wiped out.
narration: When children are forced into a rigid six-hour school day, it stifles their spirit and creativity, wiping out the natural enthusiasm they have as young learners.

author: John Holt
text: In fact, most of what happens in school makes children feel the exact opposite--stupid, incompetent, ashamed.
narration: In reality, school often leaves children feeling the opposite of empowered; they feel stupid, incompetent, and ashamed.

author: John Holt
text: We think school can do something for us that we can't do ourselves.
narration: Many believe that school offers something we can't achieve on our own.

author: John Holt
text: If you have the need, the answer is always there, too.
narration: When there's a genuine need to learn, the answers will always present themselves.

author: John Holt
text: The fact is, however, that most schools are far more concerned to have children accept the values of mass society than to help them resist them. When school people hear about people teaching their children at home, they almost always say, 'But aren't you afraid that your children are going to grow up to be different, outsiders, misfits, unable to adjust to society?' They take it for granted that in order to live reasonably happily, usefully, and successfully in the world you have to be mostly like most other people.
narration: Most schools prioritize having children accept the values of mass society rather than helping them resist those values. When educators hear about homeschooling, they often express concern that children might grow up to be outsiders or misfits, assuming that to thrive in life, one must conform to the majority.

author: John Holt
text: If there were no other reason for wanting to keep kids out of school, the social life would be reason enough. In all but a very few of the schools I have taught in, visited, or know anything about, the social life of the children is mean-spirited, competitive, exclusive, status-seeking, snobbish...
narration: The social life in most schools is often mean-spirited and competitive. In fact, keeping kids out of school could be justified solely based on the negative aspects of their social interactions.

author: John Holt
text: The richer the families the children come from, the worse these traits seem to be.
narration: Interestingly, it appears that the wealthier the families of the children, the more pronounced these negative social traits become.

author: John Holt
text: Children who spend almost all their time in groups of other people their own age, shut out of society's serious work and concerns, with almost no contact with any adults except child-watchers, are going to feel that what 'all the other kids' are doing is the right, the best, the only thing to do.
narration: When children are isolated in peer groups and lack meaningful adult interactions, they often come to believe that whatever their peers do is the only acceptable behavior.

author: John Holt
text: These are clearly not things that one has to spend three years talking about. And in fact these are not what schools of education talk about. They give very little thought to the act of teaching itself--helping another person find something out, or answering that person's questions. What they spend most of their time doing is preparing their students to work in the strange world of schools--which, in all fairness, is what the students want to find out: how to get a teaching job and keep it.
narration: It's clear that these are not topics that require years of discussion. In fact, schools of education rarely focus on the essence of teaching—helping someone discover new knowledge or answering their questions. Instead, they primarily prepare students for navigating the peculiar environment of schools, which is what many students are eager to learn: how to secure and maintain a teaching position.

author: John Holt
text: Scaring people only blocks learning.
narration: Fear only hinders the learning process.

author: John Holt
text: A child teaching another is not disappointed if the other does not understand or learn, since teaching is not his main work and he is not worried about whether he is or is not a good teacher. He may be exasperated, may even say, 'Come on, dummy, pay attention, what's the matter with you?' Since children tend to be direct and blunt with each other anyway, this probably won't bother the learner.
narration: When a child teaches another, they aren't disappointed if the other struggles to understand. Teaching isn't their primary role, and they don't stress about being a 'good teacher.' They might express frustration directly, but this honesty often doesn't deter the learner.

author: John Holt
text: People do not need a Ph. D. or some kind of certificate to help their children find their way into the world.
narration: You don't need a Ph.D. or a certificate to guide your children in navigating the world.

author: John Holt
text: There is in Michigan a man named Ovshinsky who stood solid-state physics on its ear by inventing a theory by which noncrystalline substances could be used to do things which, according to orthodox theory, only crystalline materials could do. For a number of years orthodox physicists dismissed Ovshinsky's ideas. But he was able to demonstrate them so clearly in laboratory experiments that they were finally obliged to admit that he was right. Ovshinsky never finished high school. There are probably more cases like this than we know, and there would be a great many more except for compulsory schooling laws.
narration: In Michigan, there's a man named Ovshinsky who revolutionized solid-state physics with a theory that challenged conventional wisdom. Despite being dismissed for years, he proved his ideas through clear laboratory experiments, yet he never finished high school. This raises the question: how many more like him exist, hindered by compulsory schooling laws?

author: John Holt
text: It is a kind of Catch-22 situation to say, first, that all children have to spend all that time in schools, and then to say that all kinds of things can only be learned in schools.
narration: It's a Catch-22: we say children must spend all their time in school, yet claim that only in schools can they learn.

author: John Holt
text: Your business is, as far as you can, to help them realize their human potential, and to that end you put as much as you can of good into their lives, and keep out as much as you can of bad. If you think--as you do--that school is bad, then it is clear what you should do.
narration: As a parent, your role is to nurture your children's potential by enriching their lives with positive experiences and shielding them from negativity. If you believe school is detrimental, the path forward is clear.

author: John Holt
text: To learn to know oneself, and to find a life worth living and work worth doing, is problem and challenge enough, without having to waste time on the fake and unworthy challenges of school.
narration: Learning to understand oneself and discovering meaningful work is challenging enough without the distractions of school’s trivial demands.

author: John Holt
text: Compulsory school attendance laws were invented by rich people and aimed at poor kids. These rich people said in effect, 'We educated people are perfectly capable of teaching our own children, but you poor people don't give a damn about your kids and don't know enough to teach them anything if you wanted to.'
narration: Compulsory school attendance laws were created by the wealthy, targeting the poor. They essentially claimed, 'We can teach our kids, but you can't even care for yours.'

author: John Holt
text: there's nothing special or even necessary about schools and that more children would probably be better off if they weren't forced to go. Our older son, a voracious reader then in the sixth grade, was bored in the classroom because almost everything that was taught he had already discovered on his own. For him school was a long review and an authoritarian prison which sapped his strength.
narration: There's nothing special or even necessary about schools, and many children might be better off if they weren't forced to attend. Our older son, a voracious reader in sixth grade, found school boring because he had already discovered most of the material on his own. For him, school felt like a long review and an authoritarian prison that drained his energy.

author: John Holt
text: Testing itself is not necessarily an accurate indicator of a person's knowledge or capabilities.
narration: Testing isn't always an accurate measure of someone's knowledge or abilities.

author: John Holt
text: The necessity for control and discipline outweighs the energy devoted to discovering and meeting each child's needs. A natural approach to children's learning does not force facts and skills on them before they are ready, but allows their own interests and talents to lead them into areas of knowledge.
narration: The need for control and discipline often overshadows the effort to understand and meet each child's unique needs. A natural approach to learning doesn't impose facts and skills before a child is ready; instead, it lets their interests and talents guide them into new areas of knowledge.

author: John Holt
text: Education is not something that should be done to you, but something that you do.
narration: Education isn't just something that happens to you; it's something you actively engage in.

author: John Holt
text: Learning goes on every hour of the day, so how can you grade or test that accumulated knowledge?
narration: Learning happens all day long, so how can we possibly grade or test all that knowledge?

author: John Holt
text: Once a child starts school, the home becomes school-centered, not family-centered.
narration: When a child enters school, the focus shifts from family to school, making home less about family and more about education.

author: John Holt
text: Education is not something that should be done to you, but something that you do.
narration: Education isn't just something that happens to you; it's something you actively engage in.

author: John Holt
text: The school tries to match learning and individual development but this is impossible in a classroom of 20 students who all make demands on one teacher.
narration: Schools attempt to align learning with individual growth, but this is unfeasible in a classroom filled with twenty students, each with their own needs.

author: John Holt
text: Learning goes on every hour of the day, so how can you grade or test that accumulated knowledge?
narration: Learning happens continuously throughout the day, so how can we possibly grade or test all that knowledge?

author: John Holt
text: But the schools, and many adults outside of school, hardly ever recognize or honor such motives, can hardly even imagine that they exist. In their place they put Greed and Fear. How can unschoolers revive in their children those earlier, deeper, richer sources of human action? It is not easy. Perhaps the only thing to do is to be patient and wait. After all, if we do not constantly re-injure our bodies, in time they usually heal themselves.
narration: Schools often overlook the natural curiosity and desire for mastery in children, replacing these with greed and fear. Unschoolers face the challenge of reviving these deeper motivations, requiring patience and faith in the healing of the human spirit.

author: John Holt
text: What children need is not new and better curricula but access to more and more of the real world; plenty of time and space to think over their experiences, and to use fantasy and play to make meaning out of them; and advice, road maps, guidebooks, to make it easier for them to get where they want to go (not where we think they ought to go), and to find out what they want to find out.
narration: Children require access to the real world rather than just improved curricula. They need time and space to reflect on their experiences and to explore their interests freely, guided by their own desires.

author: John Holt
text: These truants rarely brush with the law. Their trademark is their solitude. ... They fear both worlds. They sense the futility of the jobs that are available even for those who do finish high school. ... Most of them are intelligent, sensitive children, far more accomplished in the arts of reading and mathematics than their peers who either attend the schools or lurk outside of them.
narration: Truants often avoid trouble with the law, marked by their solitude and a deep awareness of the futility in both school and street life. Many are intelligent and skilled, often surpassing their peers in reading and math.

author: John Holt
text: If this was so, it means that to punish him for cutting classes the school lied about her academic work--an outrageous and I would think, if tested in court, probably illegal practice which is common in schools all over the country.
narration: Punishing students for skipping classes often leads schools to misrepresent their academic performance, a practice that could be deemed illegal if challenged.

author: John Holt
text: How easy it would be, and how much less expensive than running giant schools, and jails for those who won't go to them, to find ways to help and encourage the interests and talents of these children, and many others like them.
narration: Imagine the simplicity and cost-effectiveness of supporting children's interests and talents instead of maintaining large schools and jails for those who resist them.

author: John Holt
text: But the school must not try to compel learning. If it does, people will argue endlessly and furiously about what kinds of learning must be compelled.
narration: However, schools should not force learning. If they do, it leads to endless arguments about what should be required.

author: John Holt
text: Any kid who has ever been to school knows that 'school' is only a special name for a kid's jail, and it's hard for them to imagine having fun, doing what they want, or being creative in jail.
narration: Kids who have experienced school often see it as a kind of prison, making it difficult for them to envision fun, freedom, or creativity.

author: John Holt
text: What we need in our communities is not so much schools as a variety of protected, safe, interesting spaces where children can gather, meet and make friends, and do things together.
narration: What our communities truly need are diverse, safe spaces for children to gather, connect, and engage in activities together.

author: John Holt
text: Here I am talking about them learning without teaching --learning by doing, by wondering, by figuring things out, and often in the process resisting teaching when well-meaning adults try to force it on them.
narration: In this section, I discuss how children learn without formal teaching—through doing, wondering, and figuring things out, often resisting attempts by well-meaning adults to impose teaching on them.

author: John Holt
text: It's a waste of time and quickly degenerates into intellectual bullying to try to sidetrack a kid onto topics you think he should be learning.
narration: Trying to force a child onto topics you believe they should learn is not only a waste of time, but it can also lead to intellectual bullying.

author: John Holt
text: Some years ago I was reading aloud to a small child, as yet a nonreader, perhaps three or four years old.
narration: I recall reading aloud to a small child, who was not yet reading themselves.

author: John Holt
text: A word to the wise is infuriating! Yes it is, because it is insulting, and little children pick up this expression of (often loving and protective) distrust or contempt, even when we're not conscious of sending it.
narration: The phrase 'a word to the wise' can be infuriating and insulting to children, who sense the distrust or contempt behind it, even if we don't realize we're conveying that.

author: John Holt
text: Since to so many people 'learning' means what happens in school, or what is supposed to happen, I would rather use other words to describe what we humans do as a natural part of our living. 'Finding out' seems to fit pretty well.
narration: Many equate learning with what occurs in school, but I prefer to describe our natural process of discovery as 'finding out.'

author: John Holt
text: This little incident tells us more about the true nature of children (and so, all humans) than fifty years worth of Pavlovian behaviorist or Skinnerian operant conditioning experiments. Maybe Psychologist and Pigeon is a good game, for a while at least. But all human beings soon want to play Psychologist; no one wants to be the Pigeon. We humans are not by nature like sheep or pigeons, unquestioning, docile, happy to work the machine as long as it lights up its green lights or rolls out its food pellets. Like these children, we want to find out how the machine works, and then work it. We want to find out how things happen, so that we can make them happen. That is the kind of creature we are.
narration: This incident reveals more about the true nature of children than decades of behaviorist experiments. While some may enjoy being tested, humans naturally want to understand and manipulate their environment. We are not passive like sheep or pigeons; we seek to discover how things work so we can make them happen.

author: John Holt
text: It taught me an understanding of how the world works, so that I could figure out what I did not actually know. I recall what I did in fifth grade to free up more time to study airplanes, which I was then immersed in as a subject. The teacher wanted us to come up with five new words a week which we were supposed to define as a vocabulary lesson. Trouble was, words did not come to me at this steady pace. So, one day, I reached into the dictionary for two hundred-odd words and did a year's assignments in one bored stroke. Then I went back to gobbling up new and historical words as part of the new book I was writing on airplanes.
narration: I learned how the world works, allowing me to identify gaps in my knowledge. In fifth grade, when tasked with defining five new words each week, I grew frustrated. So, I quickly completed a year's worth of assignments in one go, freeing myself to dive deeper into my passion for airplanes.

author: John Holt
text: To parents I say, above all else, don't let your home become some terrible miniature copy of the school. No lesson plans! No quizzes! No tests! No report cards! Even leaving your children alone would be better; at least they could figure out some things on their own. Live together, as well as you can; enjoy life together, as much as you can. Ask questions to find out something about the world itself, not to find out whether or not someone knows it.
narration: I urge parents not to let their homes turn into a miniature version of school. Avoid lesson plans, quizzes, tests, and report cards. Sometimes, leaving children to their own devices is better; they might discover things on their own. Focus on living and enjoying life together, and ask questions to learn about the world, not just to test knowledge.

author: John Holt
text: The most important question any thinking creature can ask itself is, 'What is worth thinking about?'
narration: The key question for any thinking being is, 'What is worth thinking about?'

author: John Holt
text: When we deny its right to decide that for itself, when we try to control what it must attend to and think about, we make it less observant, resourceful, and adaptive, in a word, less intelligent, in a blunter word, more stupid.
narration: When we take away the right to choose what to think about, we hinder observation, resourcefulness, and adaptability, ultimately making one less intelligent.

author: John Holt
text: The reason my work with the leaves and worms was interesting and exciting to those boys was precisely that it was my work, something I was doing for my good, not theirs. It was not some sort of 'project' that I had cooked up because I thought they might be interested in it. I wasn't out there raking up leaves in the hope that some children might see me and want to join in. I never asked them to help, never even hinted; they instead on helping me.
narration: The reason my work with the leaves and worms was so interesting and exciting to those boys was that it was my work, something I was doing for my own good, not theirs. It wasn't a project I created hoping they would be interested. I wasn't raking leaves hoping children would see me and want to join in. I never asked them to help, nor did I hint at it; they insisted on helping me.

author: John Holt
text: I hate to think of them ever becoming like the bored, sullen, angry, destructive teenagers who hang out every day at the Boylston Street entrance to the Public Garden.
narration: I dread the thought of them becoming like the bored, sullen, angry teenagers who loiter every day at the Boylston Street entrance to the Public Garden.

author: John Holt
text: What you really mean is, you want to work on children. You want to do things to them, or for them--wonderful things, no doubt--which you think will help them. What's more, you want to do these things whether the children want them done or not.
narration: What you really mean is that you want to work on children. You want to do things to them or for them—wonderful things, no doubt—that you believe will help them, regardless of whether the children want those things done.

author: John Holt
text: What they will teach is that there is no one best way, and that it is a waste of time and energy to look for it; that children (like adults) learn in a great many different ways; that each child learns best in the ways that most interest, excite, and satisfy her or him; and that the business of school should be to offer to learners the widest possible range of choices, both in what to learn and ways to learn it.
narration: What they will teach is that there isn't just one best way to learn. It's a waste of time to look for it. Children, like adults, learn in many different ways, and each child learns best in ways that interest and excite them. Schools should offer the widest range of choices in what and how to learn.

author: John Holt
text: Because they don't understand natural authority, the schools are in an authority crisis. Their coercive authority breaks down more and more, but it is the only kind they know.
narration: Schools are facing an authority crisis because they don't understand natural authority. Their coercive methods are failing, yet it's the only approach they know.

author: John Holt
text: Most people (particularly discerning parents) are convinced that our present educational system is not only mindless, but harmful as well. It damages children and ruthlessly suppresses their creative urges and individual identities. The most successful students are also the most dull and unimaginative, as efficiency in learning is exclusively identified with cramming.
narration: Many discerning parents believe our current educational system is not just mindless, but harmful. It damages children, suppressing their creativity and individuality. The most successful students often turn out to be the dullest, as efficiency in learning is equated with cramming.

author: John Holt
text: Education, with its supporting system of compulsory and competitive schooling, all its carrots and sticks, its grades, diplomas, and credentials, now seems to me perhaps the most authoritarian and dangerous of all the social inventions of mankind.
narration: Education, supported by compulsory schooling and its grades and diplomas, appears to be one of the most authoritarian and dangerous social inventions.

author: John Holt
text: My concern is not to improve 'education' but to do away with it, to end the ugly and antihuman business of people-shaping and let people shape themselves.
narration: I'm not concerned with improving education; I want to abolish it and end the harmful practice of shaping people, allowing them to shape themselves instead.

author: John Holt
text: The chances are we will have universal compulsory education and compulsory schools for at least another generation. Do not waste your energy trying to reform all these schools. They cannot be reformed.
narration: It's likely that we'll have universal compulsory education for at least another generation. Don't waste your energy trying to reform these schools; they cannot be reformed.

author: John Holt
text: We need to reveal as untrue—as myths, illusions, and lies—the stories and alibis the schools and the educators tell us to justify themselves and explain their repeated failures.
narration: We must expose the myths and lies that schools and educators use to justify their failures.

author: John Holt
text: Thus, one of the stupidest things the S-chools do is insist that children 'comprehend' everything they read, and read only what they comprehend. People who read well do not learn to read this way. They learn by plunging into books that are 'too hard' for them, enjoying what they can understand, wondering and guessing about what they do not, and not worrying when they cannot find an answer.
narration: One of the most misguided practices in schools is the insistence that children must fully comprehend everything they read, only engaging with texts they understand. In reality, proficient readers often dive into challenging books, relishing what they grasp, pondering the unknown, and not stressing over unanswered questions.

author: John Holt
text: The most valuable and indeed essential asset the student brings to any learning task is a willingness to adventure, to take risks.
narration: The most crucial asset a student brings to learning is their willingness to explore and take risks.

author: John Holt
text: If the child reads only when he wants to, and asks for help only when he feels he needs it, he will work at full capacity, throw himself into the task instead of away from it, and rarely need help at all.
narration: When children read only when they choose to and seek help as needed, they engage fully with the task and seldom require assistance.

author: John Holt
text: School is just this sort of compulsory-treatment institution. Society has decided that one group of people, teachers, shall do all sorts of things to another group of people, the students, whether they want it or not, until the teachers think the students measure up, know enough about the world to go out and live in it.
narration: School functions as a compulsory treatment institution, where society has determined that teachers will impose their will on students until they believe the students are ready to face the world.

author: John Holt
text: In this community the children live their lives as they see fit. Subject only—like all of us—to the limits that they do not hurt each other, or destroy or unduly damage each others’ or common property, the children in this school do what they want, with whom they want, for as long as they want, and all of the time.
narration: In this community, children are free to live their lives as they choose, limited only by the need to respect one another and their shared spaces.

author: John Holt
text: It is not possible to spend any prolonged period visiting public school classrooms without being appalled by the mutilation visible everywhere—mutilation of spontaneity, of joy in learning, or pleasure in creating, or sense of self. . . . Because adults take the schools so much for granted, they fail to appreciate what grim, joyless places most American schools are, how oppressive and petty are the rules by which they are governed, how intellectually sterile and esthetically barren the atmosphere, what an appalling lack of civility obtains on the part of teachers and principals, what contempt they unconsciously display for students as students.
narration: Visiting public school classrooms for any length of time reveals a shocking reality—the mutilation of spontaneity, joy in learning, and creativity. Adults often overlook how grim and joyless most American schools are, governed by oppressive rules and an atmosphere that is intellectually sterile and aesthetically barren, where teachers and principals display contempt for students.

author: John Holt
text: Compulsory and competitive schools are cruel by their very nature. . . . Many of them were badly hurt by the experience. To this day they have not recovered, and many never fully recover, from that school’s sustained (however unintended) attack on their dignity, independence, and self-esteem.
narration: Compulsory and competitive schools are inherently cruel. Many children suffer deeply from their experiences, and some never fully recover from the attack on their dignity, independence, and self-esteem.

author: John Holt
text: Even if it were true, as in some cases it may be, that S-chools today are somewhat less cruel, painful, fearful, and humiliating than they used to be, they are more harmful in other ways. They take much more of children’s time, and more all the time. They give children less and less time to live their own lives, pursue their own interests, or perhaps find ways outside of school to make up for the failures, fears, and boredom of school.
narration: Even if schools today are somewhat less cruel, they are more harmful in other ways. They consume more of children's time, leaving them less opportunity to live their own lives and pursue their interests, often forcing them to compensate for the failures and boredom experienced in school.

author: John Holt
text: What is being asked for, and rewarded, and so trained, are fake smiles, fake laughter, fake teacher-pleasing behavior.
narration: What schools demand and reward are fake smiles, fake laughter, and behavior that pleases teachers.

author: John Holt
text: The children are not learning, never did learn, most of what the S-chools say they are T-eaching. The question we must ask, then, is what are they teaching, what are they really for? ... The first task is to shut young people out of adult society.
narration: Children aren't really learning what schools claim to teach. We need to question what schools are actually doing. Their primary task seems to be keeping young people away from adult society.

author: John Holt
text: A much more important and indeed essential social function of S-chools is ranking—that is, grading and labeling, putting children into pecking orders, dividing them into winners and losers. ... The S-chools, then, must run a race which mostly rich kids will win but which most poor people will accept as fair.
narration: Schools primarily serve to rank students, creating a divide between winners and losers. They set up a system where mostly affluent kids succeed, while the majority of poorer students are led to believe it's a fair competition.

author: John Holt
text: Fear blocks learning. The skillful learner must trust the world, and himself to be able to cope with it. ... But whatever it is that happens, fear stops it from happening.
narration: Fear hinders learning. To learn effectively, students must trust themselves and their environment. Yet, fear can prevent this essential process from occurring.

author: John Holt
text: S-chools say, and many S-chool people believe, that they really want all children to succeed, to learn all that the S-chools are trying to teach them. But if someday, somewhere, a T-eacher ever did the job he was paid to do, and got all his kids to learn all the stuff he was teaching, he would have to give them all A’s.
narration: Schools claim they want every child to succeed, but if a teacher actually achieved that, they'd be pressured to raise standards and challenge students more.

author: John Holt
text: The S-chools, by taking the power to make choices from their students, corrupt them. It is as if, in making ourselves into interchangeable mechanical parts of institutions, we had transferred to them our very souls. The institutions live, hunger, thirst, suffer, and die. The people are robots.
narration: Schools take away students' power to make choices, corrupting them in the process. It's as if we've turned ourselves into interchangeable parts of institutions, transferring our very souls to them. The institutions live and die, while the people become mere robots.

author: John Holt
text: They must not be jails for the young; in short, they must not, for any age, be compulsory. And they must not be allowed to rank and label their students.
narration: Schools should not be prisons for the young; they must not be compulsory for any age, nor should they rank or label students.

author: John Holt
text: It should be a punishable offense for a S-chool, or any of its governors, administrators, employees, consultants, or agents, to make, keep, or circulate such records about children.
narration: It should be illegal for schools or their staff to create, maintain, or share records about children.

author: John Holt
text: If the S-chool wants to give a child other sorts of tests—psychological, medical, or whatever, including IQ tests—they should be able to do so only with the parents’ permission, and must send them the results of those tests.
narration: Schools should only administer psychological or medical tests with parental permission, and they must share the results with parents.

author: John Holt
text: The S-chools have basically bad purposes; they cannot be made good places for children, for teaching, learning, intelligence, or growth, until those purposes are taken away from them; and these purposes cannot be taken away by people working in S-chools, but only by the general public.
narration: Schools have fundamentally flawed purposes that cannot be transformed into nurturing environments for teaching and learning until those purposes are removed, and that change can only come from the broader public, not from within the schools themselves.

author: John Holt
text: To the extent they took us seriously they would say, 'Why do I have to sit here listening to you?'
narration: If students took us seriously, they'd question why they have to sit and listen to us.

author: John Holt
text: The idea of a 'radical teacher' is absurd.
narration: The concept of a 'radical teacher' is fundamentally flawed.

author: John Holt
text: As long as S-chools remain S-chools—compulsory, coercive, competitive—any changes we make in them will not go very deep or very far, or last for very long.
narration: As long as schools continue to be compulsory, coercive, and competitive, any changes we implement will be superficial and temporary.

author: John Holt
text: Meanwhile, education—compulsory schooling, compulsory learning—is a tyranny and a crime against the human mind and spirit. Let all those escape it who can, any way they can.
narration: In the meantime, compulsory schooling and learning represent a tyranny and a crime against the human mind and spirit. Let everyone who can find a way to escape it.

author: John Holt
text: I have been very critical of what we might call 'schoolism,' but I had not thought of it as being anywhere near as harmful as, say, militarism or modern nationalism, and would have considered such comparisons overdrawn. They now seem altogether apt and exact. This raises a kind of ethical dilemma.
narration: I've often criticized what we call 'schoolism,' but I never thought it was as harmful as militarism or nationalism. Now, those comparisons feel entirely appropriate, raising an ethical dilemma.

author: John Holt
text: If you feel this, then indeed you should feel guilty if you go on anyway and get a diploma simply for the advantages it will give you over the people who can’t get one.
narration: If you believe this, you should feel guilty for pursuing a diploma just for the advantages it gives you over those who can't obtain one.

author: John Holt
text: Institutions do not occupy all the life space on earth.
narration: Institutions don't take up all the space in our lives.

author: John Holt
text: Our life space is always either expanding or contracting, and it only expands when we try to expand it. So we must be continually driving bargains with whatever circumstances, and they may be institutions, limit our life space.
narration: Our life space is either expanding or contracting, and it only grows when we actively seek to expand it. We must constantly negotiate with the circumstances, including institutions, that limit our freedom.

author: John Holt
text: You can hardly consistently or honestly urge others to give up the ticket you were glad and eager to take.
narration: It's hard to genuinely encourage others to give up the ticket you were eager to obtain.

author: John Holt
text: You will probably not like the fixed curricula or homogeneous grouping, or the necessity for giving exams, or the disciplinary rules, or the psychological probing, or the making of dossiers on children to give to their future employers. In short, with respect to most of the things that I think are deeply and fundamentally wrong with schools, a large number of you are in a large agreement with me.
narration: You might find the fixed curricula, homogeneous grouping, and the need to give exams, along with disciplinary rules and psychological probing, quite unappealing. In fact, many of you likely agree with me about the fundamental issues plaguing our schools.

author: John Holt
text: You simply cannot imagine the number of teachers I have met or heard from that say to me in one way or another, 'I’ve been trying for years and years to find a place in which I can work, with children in the way you suggest.'
narration: It's astonishing how many teachers I've encountered who express their frustration, saying they've spent years searching for a place where they can work with children in the way I advocate.

author: John Holt
text: You have no right to a life of your own.
narration: You essentially have no right to live your own life.

author: John Holt
text: I think that any small-scale changes and reforms that we make in schools are perilous and likely to be short-lived as long as the fundamental mission of the schools is inhumane, as I think it is. That is, as long as schools are primarily in the jail business, the indoctrination business, the business of separating children into winners and losers, there is going to be a very real limit to what can be done to make them really helpful places.
narration: I believe that any small-scale changes we make in schools are risky and likely to be short-lived as long as their fundamental mission remains inhumane. As long as schools operate like jails, indoctrinating students and separating them into winners and losers, there will be a real limit to how helpful they can truly be.

author: John Holt
text: I think we have to understand why many young people break away from the culture they grew up in, and here I have to say that I entirely agree with them, that the prevailing culture is exploitative, unjust, inhuman and above all suicidal—to break away from this takes so much strength and energy and leaves so many psychic injuries that there’s not much left over to make something different.
narration: We need to understand why many young people choose to break away from the culture they grew up in. I completely agree with them that the prevailing culture is exploitative, unjust, inhuman, and ultimately suicidal. Breaking away from this takes immense strength and energy, often leaving little left to create something new.

author: John Holt
text: I push for alternatives to schools, escapes from schools, and to a large degree elimination of schools, certainly of compulsory schooling, because the schools look to me like a burning building. If you see someone in a burning building you know that the longer he stays in there the worse he’s going to get hurt, and you want to get him out and provide ways for him to get out.
narration: I advocate for alternatives to schools and even the elimination of compulsory schooling because, to me, schools resemble a burning building. When you see someone trapped in a burning building, you know that the longer they stay, the worse they will be hurt. You want to help them escape and find a way out.

author: John Holt
text: I have become totally and intransigently opposed to compulsory schooling, or indeed any and all forms of learning based on coercion, bribe, and threat. To take away from someone their control of their own learning, their exploration of the world, now seems to me a terrible crime against the human mind and spirit.
narration: I've become completely opposed to compulsory schooling and any form of learning that relies on coercion, bribery, or threats. Taking away a person's control over their own learning and exploration feels like a grave injustice against the human mind and spirit.

author: John Holt
text: To make schools truly non-compulsory and to give young people a true choice of whether to attend them or not, we must do much more than abolish compulsory attendance laws. We must take from the schools altogether the power they now have to grade, rank, and label their students, to make judgments about them which will follow them throughout their lives and to a very large degree determine what they can do and become.
narration: To truly make schools non-compulsory and give young people a real choice about attending, we need to do much more than just remove attendance laws. We must strip schools of their power to grade, rank, and label students, as these judgments can significantly shape their futures.

author: John Holt
text: The most important reason for making schools truly voluntary is that they would have to begin to learn to do what they have never done, help people to learn what they truly wanted to learn, and make available to people ideas and skills that were truly useful and important in society, and all this efficiently and at a reasonable cost. I don’t believe in schools. It’s not just that I don’t believe they are reformable. I don’t believe they are needed. I don’t believe they were a good invention in the first place.
narration: The key reason for making schools truly voluntary is that they would need to learn to help people discover what they genuinely want to learn, providing useful and important ideas and skills efficiently and affordably. I don't believe in schools; I don't think they can be reformed or that they are necessary. In fact, I question whether they were a good invention to begin with.

author: John Holt
text: I think the very idea that what we learn outside of regular life is better and more important than what we learn [in it] is mistaken, harmful, discriminatory, class-prejudiced, etc.
narration: I believe the notion that what we learn outside of everyday life is superior or more significant than what we learn within it is fundamentally flawed and harmful.

author: John Holt
text: An absolutely sure-fire, 100% guaranteed recipe for making an institutional Dumb Class is to set up an institution which is not held responsible for the quality of its product, or work. Education is the #1 example.
