narration: A guaranteed way to create an institutional 'Dumb Class' is to establish an institution that isn't held accountable for the quality of its output. Education is the prime example of this.

author: John Holt
text: A life worth living and work worth doing—that is what I want for children (and all people), not just, or not even, something called 'a better education.'
narration: What I truly desire for children, and for everyone, is a life worth living and work worth doing, rather than merely a so-called 'better education.'

author: John Holt
text: I don’t think, of all the many harmful and destructive things that most schools do these days, anything is more harmful and destructive than the kind of pop psychology which they practice on their students.
narration: Among the many harmful practices in schools today, I believe nothing is more destructive than the pop psychology they impose on students.

author: John Holt
text: What students need more than anything else is practice.
narration: Above all, students need practice.

author: John Holt
text: The things we do to achieve and maintain the appearance of control are often destructive of any real discipline and authority.
narration: Often, the measures we take to create an illusion of control undermine true discipline and authority.

author: Ivan Illich
text: The major obstacle on the way to a society that truly educates was well defined by a black friend of mine in Chicago, who told me that our imagination was 'all schooled up.' We permit the state to ascertain the universal educational deficiencies of its citizens and establish one specialized agency to treat them. We thus share in the delusion that we can distinguish between what is necessary education for others and what is not, just as former generations established laws which defined what was sacred and what was profane.
narration: A friend of mine once pointed out that our imagination is 'all schooled up.' We let the state determine the educational deficiencies of its citizens and create agencies to address them. This leads us to believe we can differentiate between necessary education for others and what isn't, much like past generations defined sacred and profane.

author: Ivan Illich
text: School is radically divisive in a similar way.
narration: School creates divisions in society.

author: Ivan Illich
text: The claim that a liberal society can be founded on the modern school is paradoxical. The safeguards of individual freedom are all canceled in the dealings of a teacher with his pupil.
narration: It's paradoxical to think a liberal society can be built on modern schooling, as the freedoms of individuals are often stripped away in the interactions between teachers and students.

author: Ivan Illich
text: School instructs them in their own inferiority through the tax collector who makes them pay for it, or through the demagogue who raises their expectations of it.
narration: School teaches students to feel inferior, whether through taxes or inflated expectations.

author: A.S. Neill
text: Obviously, a school that makes active children sit at desks studying mostly useless subjects is a bad school. It is a good school only for those who believe in such a school, for those uncreative citizens who want docile, uncreative children who will fit into a civilization whose standard of success is money. Summerhill began as an experimental school. It is no longer such; it is now a demonstration school, for it demonstrates that freedom works.
narration: It's clear that a school forcing active children to sit at desks and study mostly useless subjects is a bad school. It's only good for those who want docile, uncreative children who fit into a society that values money above all. Summerhill started as an experiment, but now it stands as a demonstration that freedom truly works.

author: A.S. Neill
text: The function of the child is to live his own life—not the life that his anxious parents think he should live, nor a life according to the purpose of the educator who thinks she knows what is best. All this interference and guidance on the part of adults only produces a generation of robots.
narration: The child's role is to live their own life—not the one their anxious parents envision or the one dictated by educators who think they know best. This constant interference from adults only creates a generation of robots.

author: A.S. Neill
text: Indeed, the absence of fear is the finest thing that can happen to a child.
narration: Truly, the absence of fear is the greatest gift we can give a child.

author: A.S. Neill
text: Learning in itself is not as important as personality and character.
narration: It's not just about learning; personality and character matter far more.

author: A.S. Neill
text: Only an enlightened and happy home can counteract the baleful indoctrination and deadness of the usual school. The saddest letters I write are in reply to boys and girls who write telling me how they hate their schools. I answer saying that, however much they hate the lessons and the way they are presented, the damn system is there, and the only thing to do is to grit your teeth and accept it and get through it as quickly as possible.
narration: An enlightened and happy home is the only antidote to the harmful indoctrination of the typical school system. I often receive sad letters from students expressing their hatred for school. I tell them that despite their feelings, the system is in place, and they must endure it and push through as best they can.

author: A.S. Neill
text: Call it what you will, but don't call it education!
narration: You can label it however you like, but don't dare call it education!

author: A.S. Neill
text: To persecute children in this way is appalling. Young lives crushed by undeveloped, pip-squeak authorities! A natural love of learning warped by dull teaching! It makes one cry.
narration: It's shocking to see children persecuted like this. Their natural love for learning is stifled by uninspired teaching, and it truly is heartbreaking.

author: A.S. Neill
text: The awful tragedy is that the mass of children accept the insane standards of the schools, but at the terrible price of losing their inner freedom. Our schools produce a race of dead souls who are at the mercy of the politicians, the war-makers, and the profit-seekers.
narration: It's tragic that so many children accept the insane standards of schools, paying the heavy price of losing their inner freedom. Our education system creates a generation of dead souls, vulnerable to the whims of politicians, war-makers, and profit-seekers.

author: A.S. Neill
text: Most subjects in school are a pure waste of youth's valuable time.
narration: Many school subjects are simply a waste of young people's precious time.

author: A.S. Neill
text: Freedom gives children guts; unpressured children can face difficulties when they have to.
narration: Freedom empowers children; those who aren't pressured can confront challenges when necessary.

author: A.S. Neill
text: A wish to succeed must come from within. I know a boy of 19-not one of my pupils--whose father is a doctor. The boy says he also wants to be a doctor. But he cannot concentrate on anatomy and physiology; his mind is on cars, and he would really like to run a garage.
narration: Desire for success must come from within. I know a 19-year-old whose father is a doctor. He claims he wants to be a doctor too, but his true passion lies in cars; he dreams of running a garage.

author: Peter Gray
text: Nature does not turn off this enormous desire and capacity to learn when children turn five or six. We turn it off with our coercive system of schooling. The biggest, most enduring lesson of school is that learning is work, to be avoided when possible.
narration: Nature doesn't stop children's desire and ability to learn as they grow older; we do that with our coercive schooling system. The most lasting lesson they learn in school is that learning is work, something to be avoided whenever possible.

author: Peter Gray
text: To him, school was prison, and he had done nothing to deserve imprisonment.
narration: To him, school felt like a prison, and he hadn't done anything to deserve that kind of confinement.

author: Peter Gray
text: Children come into the world burning to learn and genetically programmed with extraordinary capacities for learning.
narration: Children arrive in this world eager to learn, equipped with remarkable abilities to do so.

author: Peter Gray
text: Children are designed, by nature, to play and explore on their own, independently of adults. They need freedom in order to develop; without it they suffer. The drive to play freely is a basic, biological drive. Lack of free play may not kill the physical body, as would lack of food, air, or water, but it kills the spirit and stunts mental growth.
narration: Children are naturally inclined to play and explore independently. They require freedom for their development, and without it, they suffer. The need for free play is a fundamental biological drive. While lack of play may not physically harm a child, it can damage their spirit and hinder mental growth.

author: Peter Gray
text: We have pushed children into an abnormal environment, where they are expected to spend ever greater portions of their day under adult direction, sitting at desks, listening to and reading about things that don’t interest them, and answering questions that are not their own and are not, to them, real questions.
narration: We've placed children in an unnatural environment, where they spend increasing amounts of time under adult supervision, confined to desks, engaging with topics that don't interest them, and responding to questions that feel irrelevant.

author: Peter Gray
text: All of these, in fact, interfere with children’s natural ways of learning. This is a book about children’s natural instincts to educate themselves, about the environmental conditions required for those instincts to operate optimally, and about how we, as a society, can provide those conditions at far less expense than what we currently spend on schools.
narration: These factors disrupt children's natural learning processes. This book explores children's inherent instincts to educate themselves, the conditions necessary for these instincts to thrive, and how society can create those conditions at a lower cost than our current educational system.

author: Peter Gray
text: The school system has directly and indirectly, often unintentionally, fostered an attitude in society that children learn and progress primarily by doing tasks that are directed and evaluated by adults, and that children’s own activities are wasted time.
narration: The school system has cultivated a societal belief that children learn best through adult-directed tasks and evaluations, leading to the perception that their own activities are merely a waste of time.

author: Peter Gray
text: In the name of education, we have increasingly deprived children of the time and freedom they need to educate themselves through their own means. And in the name of safety, we have deprived children of the freedom they need to develop the understanding, courage, and confidence required to face life’s dangers and challenges with equanimity.
narration: In the name of education, we've taken away the time and freedom children need to learn on their own. And in the name of safety, we've stripped them of the freedom necessary to build the understanding, courage, and confidence to face life's challenges.

author: Peter Gray
text: Children do not need more schooling. They need less schooling and more freedom.
narration: Children don't need more schooling; they need less of it and more freedom.

author: Peter Gray
text: Every time we reduce children’s opportunities for free play by increasing their time at school or at other adult-directed activities, we reduce further their opportunities to learn to control their own lives, to learn that they are not simply victims of circumstances and powerful others.
narration: Every time we limit children's chances for free play by extending their time in school or other adult-led activities, we further diminish their ability to take control of their own lives and realize they aren't just victims of circumstances.

author: Peter Gray
text: Schooling came to be seen as a state function that was essential for national security, not unlike the army. The state’s power to forcibly conscript children into schools was understood as comparable to the state’s power to conscript young men into the army.
narration: Schooling was viewed as a crucial state function for national security, similar to the army. The state's ability to forcibly conscript children into schools was seen as akin to conscripting young men into military service.

author: Peter Gray
text: The task of the student, then as now, was to be punctual and obedient, to pay attention, to complete assignments on schedule, and to memorize and feed back to the teacher the lessons taught, without questioning either their content or the prescribed methods for learning them.
narration: Students are expected to be punctual and obedient, to pay attention, complete assignments on time, and memorize lessons without questioning their content or the methods used to teach them.

author: Peter Gray
text: We have forgotten that children are designed by nature to learn through self-directed play and exploration, and so, more and more, we deprive them of freedom to learn, subjecting them instead to the tedious and painfully slow learning methods devised by those who run the schools.
narration: We've overlooked that children naturally learn through self-directed play and exploration. Instead, we deprive them of this freedom, forcing them into tedious learning methods created by those in charge of schools.

author: Peter Gray
text: Children don’t like school because, like all human beings, they crave freedom, and in school they are not free.
narration: Children dislike school because, like all humans, they yearn for freedom, and in school, they are not free.

author: Peter Gray
text: To incarcerate an adult, we must prove, in a court of law, that the person has committed a crime or is a serious threat to self or others. Yet we incarcerate children because of their age.
narration: To incarcerate an adult, we must prove they've committed a crime or pose a serious threat. Yet, we imprison children solely based on their age.

author: Peter Gray
text: The biggest, most enduring lesson of school is that learning is work, to be avoided when possible, not joyful play as children would otherwise believe.
narration: The most significant lesson children learn in school is that learning is work to be avoided, rather than the joyful play it should be.

author: Peter Gray
text: Students understand that the rules distinguishing cheating from not cheating in school are like the rules of a game. But it’s a game they did not choose to play. They have little or no say in what they study, how they are tested, or the rules concerning what is or isn’t cheating. Under these conditions, it’s hard to respect the rules.
narration: Students realize that the rules around cheating in school resemble a game, but it's one they never chose to participate in. They lack control over what they study, how they're assessed, and the definitions of cheating, making it difficult to respect those rules.

author: Peter Gray
text: The result of the pressure is that students are rewarded for being dishonest, which is really very sad. Teachers often say that if you cheat in school you are only cheating yourself, because you are shortchanging your own education. But that argument holds water only if what you would have learned by not cheating outweighs the value of whatever you did with the time saved by cheating.
narration: The pressure leads students to be rewarded for dishonesty, which is truly unfortunate. Teachers claim that cheating only cheats oneself, but this only holds true if the learning missed by cheating is more valuable than the time saved.

author: Peter Gray
text: By segregating children by age, by caging them in so they can’t avoid those who harass them, by indoctrinating them in a setting where competition and winning—being better than others—are the highest values, and by denying them any meaningful voice in school governance, we establish the breeding grounds for bullying.
narration: By separating children by age and confining them in a competitive environment that values winning above all, while denying them a voice in school governance, we create the perfect conditions for bullying.

author: Peter Gray
text: In a system in which we teachers do the grading, few students are going to criticize or even question the ideas we offer, and if we try to induce criticism by grading for it, we generate false criticism. To think critically, people must feel motivated and free to voice their own ideas and raise their own questions.
narration: In a grading system controlled by teachers, students are unlikely to challenge or question the ideas presented. Genuine critical thinking requires motivation and the freedom to express personal ideas and inquiries.

author: Peter Gray
text: The school curriculum represents a tiny subset of the skills and knowledge that are important to our society. In this day and age, nobody can learn more than a sliver of all there is to know. Why force everyone to learn the same sliver?
narration: The school curriculum only covers a small fraction of the essential skills and knowledge in our society. In today's world, no one can grasp everything, so why should everyone be forced to learn the same limited content?

author: Peter Gray
text: Greenberg began to see the university as complicit in a system of education that he could no longer tolerate. In fact, he realized, it was universities, through their education departments, that promoted the K–12 schooling policies that he deplored. A major purpose of schooling in a democracy, argued Greenberg, should be to help people prepare for the opportunities and responsibilities of democratic citizenship; and you don’t do that effectively by depriving students of those opportunities and responsibilities as they are growing up.
narration: Greenberg recognized that universities were part of a problematic educational system. He argued that schooling should prepare students for democratic citizenship, but this is undermined when students are deprived of opportunities and responsibilities during their formative years.

author: Peter Gray
text: The educational system in our country today is the most un-American institution we have in our midst. The rights of free speech, free assembly, freedom to choose one’s own path to happiness, and fair trial when accused of a misdeed are nonexistent for students. Students generally have no voice in the formation of school rules and little voice in decisions about how they will spend their time each day.
narration: The current educational system is described as fundamentally un-American, with students lacking essential rights like free speech and the ability to influence school rules or their daily activities.

author: Peter Gray
text: Adults do not control children’s education; children educate themselves.
narration: In this school, the belief is that education should be self-directed by children, free from adult control.

author: Peter Gray
text: The school fulfills, in reality, all aspects of the vision set out in the early writings of Daniel Greenberg and the other school founders.
narration: This school embodies the vision that Greenberg and the founders had for education.

author: Peter Gray
text: Most people believe that children need to sit through lessons and work hard at assigned schoolwork in order to succeed in life. In fact, to suggest otherwise borders on blasphemy. Even if some children complain or rebel, even if some must be given strong drugs so they will concentrate on their assigned schoolwork, such work, most believe, is necessary.
narration: Many believe that children must endure lessons and assigned work to succeed in life, and suggesting otherwise is often seen as blasphemous. Even when children rebel or struggle, society insists that this work is essential.

author: Peter Gray
text: They believe strongly that children learn best on their own initiative, through their own self-chosen and self-directed means.
narration: They firmly believe that children learn best when they take the initiative and direct their own learning.

author: Peter Gray
text: The only students who do not succeed at the school... are those very few whose behavior is so continuously and greatly disruptive that they are expelled.
narration: The only students who struggle at the school are those few whose disruptive behavior leads to expulsion.

author: Peter Gray
text: Self-education through play and exploration requires enormous amounts of unscheduled time—time to do whatever one wants to do, without pressure, judgment, or intrusion from authority figures.
narration: Self-education thrives on ample unscheduled time, allowing individuals to explore freely, without pressure or judgment from authority.

author: Peter Gray
text: Intellectual development occurs best in a setting where people can share ideas freely, without censorship or fear of being ostracized.
narration: Intellectual growth flourishes in environments where ideas can be shared freely, without fear of censorship or ostracism.

author: Peter Gray
text: Children in school are not free to pursue their own interests, or to pursue those interests in their self-chosen ways. Children in school are more or less continuously evaluated, and the concern for evaluation and pleasing the teacher often overrides and subverts the possibility of developing genuine interests. Curiosity, playfulness, and meaningful conversation are all thwarted in school, because they require freedom.
narration: In school, children aren't free to follow their own interests or explore them in their own ways. They're constantly evaluated, and the pressure to please teachers often stifles genuine curiosity. This environment thwarts playfulness and meaningful conversation, both of which require freedom.

author: Peter Gray
text: The directive-domineering style works no better in homes than it does in nations. Today, at least in our culture, most people are repelled by the idea of beating children into submission, whether by physical or psychological means. In today’s globalized, networked world, initiative, creativity, and self-assertion are generally valued. We see that blind obedience doesn’t work as a style of life.
narration: The directive-domineering style fails both at home and in society. Nowadays, many reject the notion of forcing children into submission, whether through physical or psychological means. In our interconnected world, we value initiative, creativity, and self-assertion, recognizing that blind obedience is not a viable way to live.

author: Peter Gray
text: The school year is longer, the sanctions for missing days of school are greater, and the activities conducted at school are more rigidly controlled than in times past. Moreover, schools today extend their influence beyond the school walls and into family life far more than in times past. There are summer reading lists, for example, and parents are supposed to make sure their children get those books and read them.
narration: Today, the school year is longer, with stricter penalties for absences, and school activities are more tightly controlled than ever. Schools now reach into family life more than before, with summer reading lists that parents are expected to enforce.

author: Peter Gray
text: The school-centric model of childhood has taken increasing hold over time and affected all aspects of children’s lives. Playgrounds are no longer places where children go and play freely with one another, but are places of coaching and teaching, led by adults.
narration: The school-centric model has increasingly influenced every aspect of children's lives. Playgrounds, once spaces for free play, have transformed into areas dominated by adult coaching and instruction.

author: Peter Gray
text: Consider Alternatives to Conventional Schooling. To be a trustful parent, you may need to find an alternative to conventional schooling for your kids, an alternative that works with, rather than against, their desire and capacity to take charge of their own education. I’ve already described the Sudbury Valley School as one such alternative. At the time I am writing this, there are at least twenty-two schools in the United States modeled after Sudbury Valley and at least fourteen in other countries. These are all private schools, but their tuitions are far less than those of regular private schools.
narration: As a trustful parent, you might need to explore alternatives to conventional schooling that align with your child's desire to take charge of their own education. For instance, the Sudbury Valley School is one such option, and there are many similar schools across the United States and beyond, often with lower tuition than traditional private schools.

author: Peter Gray
text: My optimism does not come from the education establishment. That establishment— including schools of education, the textbook and testing industries, and organizations of teachers and school administrators—is so entrenched in the status quo and its own self-interests that all it can do is push for more of the same. When it becomes clear that children aren’t learning much of what is taught in school, the hue and cry from these sources is that kids must spend even more time in school and do more schoolwork at home.
narration: My optimism doesn't stem from the education establishment, which is deeply entrenched in maintaining the status quo. When it becomes evident that children aren't learning effectively, the response is often to increase their time in school and assign more homework.

author: Peter Gray
text: The only meaningful reform is one that puts kids in charge of their own learning.
narration: True reform in education must empower children to take charge of their own learning.

author: Peter Gray
text: The movement out of schools is also facilitated by the revolution in information technology. Today anyone who can get their hands on a computer with Internet access— even street kids in India—can access the world’s entire body of knowledge and ideas, all beautifully organized and available through easy-to-use search engines.
narration: The shift away from traditional schooling is also driven by advancements in information technology. Now, anyone with internet access can tap into a vast array of knowledge and ideas, making learning more accessible than ever.

author: Peter Gray
text: As more people meet adults who didn’t go to a coercive school, or who don’t send their kids to one, it will be ever harder to think of that decision as aberrant or abhorrent. And there is another force working here, too—the natural human drive for freedom and self-determination. History tells us that when people see freedom as a viable option, they choose it. When adults see that coercive schooling isn’t necessary for success in the culture, they will find it hard not to choose freedom for their kids, and the kids themselves will demand it.
narration: As more people encounter adults who didn't attend coercive schools, or who choose not to send their children to them, it will become increasingly difficult to view that choice as strange or wrong. Additionally, there's a powerful human instinct for freedom and self-determination. History shows us that when people recognize freedom as a real option, they tend to choose it. When adults realize that coercive schooling isn't essential for success in society, they will struggle to deny their children the same freedom, and the children themselves will demand it.

author: Paulo Freire
text: Education is suffering from narration sickness. The teacher talks about reality as if it were motionless, static, compartmentalized, and predictable. Or else he expounds on a topic completely alien to the existential experience of the students.
narration: Education is plagued by what Freire calls 'narration sickness.' Teachers often present reality as if it's static and predictable, or they discuss topics that are completely disconnected from the students' real-life experiences.

author: Paulo Freire
text: In the banking concept of education, knowledge is a gift bestowed by those who consider themselves knowledgeable upon those whom they consider to know nothing. Projecting an absolute ignorance onto others, a characteristic of the ideology of oppression, negates education and knowledge as processes of inquiry.
narration: Freire critiques the banking concept of education, where knowledge is seen as a gift from the knowledgeable to the ignorant, undermining true inquiry and reinforcing oppressive ideologies.

author: Paulo Freire
text: Authentic liberation—the process of humanization—is not another deposit to be made in men.
narration: True liberation is not just about depositing knowledge into individuals.

author: Paulo Freire
text: The more completely they accept the passive role imposed on them, the more they tend simply to adapt to the world as it is and to the fragmented view of reality deposited in them.
narration: The more students accept their passive roles, the more they adapt to the world as it is, accepting a fragmented view of reality.

author: Paulo Freire
text: The banking concept of education, which serves the interests of oppression, is also necrophilic.
narration: Freire argues that the banking concept of education not only supports oppression but is also necrophilic.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: Our problem in understanding forced schooling stems from an inconvenient fact: that the wrong it does from a human perspective is right from a systems perspective. Schools are supposed to teach kids their place. That’s why we have age-graded classes. In any case, it wasn’t your own little Janey or mine. Most of us tacitly accept the pragmatic terms of public school which allow every kind of psychic violence to be inflicted on Bianca in order to fulfill the prime directive of the system: putting children in their place.
narration: Understanding forced schooling is complicated by the fact that what seems wrong from a human perspective is often right from a systems perspective. Schools are designed to teach children their place, which is why we have age-graded classes. Most of us accept the terms of public schooling that allow for various forms of psychic violence to be inflicted on children to maintain this order.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: Government schooling is the most radical adventure in history. It kills the family by monopolizing the best times of childhood and by teaching disrespect for home and parents.
narration: Government schooling represents a radical shift in history, undermining family life by monopolizing childhood and instilling disrespect for home and parents.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: There isn’t a right way to become educated; there are as many ways as fingerprints.
narration: Education isn't one-size-fits-all; there are countless ways to learn, just like there are unique fingerprints.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: If you can’t be guaranteed even minimal results by these institutions, not even physical safety; if you can’t be guaranteed anything except that you’ll be arrested if you fail to surrender your kid, just what does the public in public schools mean?
narration: If these institutions can't even guarantee basic results or safety, and the only certainty is that you'll face consequences for not surrendering your child, what does 'public' really mean in public schools?

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: Ordinary people send their children to school to get smart, but what modern schooling teaches is dumbness. It’s a religious idea gone out of control. You don’t have to accept that, though, to realize this kind of economy would be jeopardized by too many smart people who understand too much.
narration: Many parents believe they send their children to school to become smart, yet modern schooling often fosters dumbness. This misguided belief is a dangerous idea, as too many intelligent individuals could threaten the existing economic order.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: Dumb people are no longer merely ignorant. Now they are indoctrinated, their minds conditioned with substantial doses of commercially prepared disinformation dispensed for tranquilizing purposes.
narration: Today, ignorance has transformed into indoctrination, where minds are conditioned with disinformation designed to keep them complacent.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: What kids dumbed down by schooling can’t do is to think for themselves or ever be at rest for very long without feeling crazy; stupefied boys and girls reveal dependence in many ways easily exploitable by their knowledgeable elders.
narration: Children who have been dumbed down by schooling struggle to think independently and often feel restless. This dependence makes them vulnerable to exploitation by those who hold knowledge.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: If you want truth, you have to struggle for it. This is my struggle.
narration: To uncover the truth, one must be willing to fight for it. This is the journey I am on.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: Every foreign commentator notes the early training in independence, the remarkable precocity of American youth, their assumption of adult responsibility. In his memoir, Tom Nichols, a New Hampshire schoolboy in the 1820s, recalls the electrifying air of expectation in early American schools: Our teachers constantly stimulated us by the glittering prizes of wealth, honors, offices, and distinctions, which were certainly within our reach—there were a hundred avenues to wealth and fame opening fair before us if we only chose to learn our lessons.
narration: Every foreign commentator has noted the early training in independence and the remarkable precocity of American youth, who assumed adult responsibilities. Tom Nichols, a New Hampshire schoolboy in the 1820s, remembered the electrifying air of expectation in early American schools. Teachers constantly stimulated students with the glittering prizes of wealth, honors, and distinctions that were well within reach, with countless avenues to wealth and fame opening up if they chose to learn their lessons.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: Something in the structure of schooling calls forth violence. While latter-day schools don’t allow energetic physical discipline, certainly they are state-of-the-art laboratories in humiliation, as your own experience should remind you. If you were to ask me now whether physical or psychological violence does more damage, I would reply that slurs, aspersion, formal ranking, insult, and inference are far and away the more deadly.
narration: There's something inherent in the structure of schooling that calls forth violence. While modern schools may not permit physical discipline, they certainly serve as laboratories for humiliation, as many can attest. If you were to ask me whether physical or psychological violence inflicts more harm, I would say that slurs, insults, and formal rankings are far more damaging.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: The way we used to be as Americans, learning everything, breaking down social class barriers, is the way we might be again without forced schooling.
narration: The way we used to be as Americans—learning everything and breaking down social class barriers—is how we could be again, if only we moved beyond forced schooling.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: The secret of American schooling is that it doesn’t teach the way children learn, and it isn’t supposed to; school was engineered to serve a concealed command economy and a deliberately re-stratified social order. It wasn’t made for the benefit of kids and families as those individuals and institutions would define their own needs. School is the first impression children get of organized society; like most first impressions, it is the lasting one.
narration: The hidden truth about American schooling is that it doesn't align with how children learn, and it wasn't designed to. Instead, it serves a concealed command economy and a deliberately stratified social order, rather than the needs of kids and families. School is the first impression children have of organized society, and like most first impressions, it sticks with them.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: The decisive dynamics which make forced schooling poisonous to healthy human development aren’t hard to spot. Work in classrooms isn’t significant work; it fails to satisfy real needs pressing on the individual; it doesn’t answer real questions experience raises in the young mind; it doesn’t contribute to solving any problem encountered in actual life. The net effect of making all schoolwork external to individual longings, experiences, questions, and problems is to render the victim listless.
narration: It's clear that forced schooling is detrimental to healthy human development. Classroom work lacks significance; it doesn't meet real needs or address the genuine questions that arise in young minds. This externalization of schoolwork leads to a sense of listlessness in students.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: Growth and mastery come only to those who vigorously self-direct.
narration: True growth and mastery are achieved only by those who take charge of their own learning.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: I don’t mean to be inflammatory, but it’s as if government schooling made people dumber, not brighter; made families weaker, not stronger; ruined formal religion with its hard-sell exclusion of God; set the class structure in stone by dividing children into classes and setting them against one another; and has been midwife to an alarming concentration of wealth and power in the hands of a fraction of the national community.
narration: I don't want to provoke unnecessarily, but it seems that government schooling has actually made people less intelligent, weakened families, undermined formal religion by excluding God, entrenched class divisions, and facilitated a troubling concentration of wealth and power among a small segment of society.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: The real gift science confers is teaching how to reach potent conclusions by common powers of observation and reasoning. But if incidental overproduction was already a crisis item in the minds of the new social planners, you can imagine what hysteria any attempt to broadcast the secrets of discovery would have occasioned.
narration: Science teaches us how to draw powerful conclusions from simple observations and reasoning. However, if overproduction was already a pressing concern for planners, any effort to share the secrets of discovery would likely have caused panic.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: The General Education Board said it best when it said children had to be organized and taught in a way that would not make them 'men of science.'
narration: The General Education Board put it plainly: children needed to be organized and taught in a way that would prevent them from becoming 'men of science.'

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: Close supervision meant radical loss of freedom from what had been known before. Now knowledge of how to do important work passed out of local possession into the hands of a few owners and managers.
narration: With close supervision came a significant loss of freedom compared to what had existed before. Knowledge about important work shifted from local hands to a select few owners and managers.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: Did a class system exist there?
narration: Was there a class system in your school?

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: Schooling was about efficiency. Social efficiency meant standardizing human units. Surprisingly enough to those who expect that institutional thinking will reflect their own thought only on a larger scale, what is an asset to a mass production economy is frequently a liability to an individual or a family. Creating value in children for a mass production workplace through schooling meant degrading their intellectual growth and discouraging any premature utility to the larger society.
narration: Schooling focused on efficiency, standardizing human units. What benefits a mass production economy often harms individuals and families. By creating value in children for a mass production workplace, schooling degraded their intellectual growth and discouraged any early contributions to society.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: The confinement of American children to warehouse schools less than a half-century later had been pioneered by the Massachusetts experiment we associate with Horace Mann in the decade just before the Civil War. Schools were the anti-matter twins of mines and mills: the latter added children to the labor market, schools subtracted them. Both were important functions of a new, centralized command economy.
narration: The confinement of American children to warehouse schools was pioneered by Massachusetts, linked to Horace Mann before the Civil War. Schools and mines served opposite functions: mines added children to the labor market, while schools took them away, both essential to a centralized command economy.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: Mass society demands tight administration, close management to an extreme degree. Humanity becomes undependable, dangerous, childlike, and suicidal under such discipline.
narration: Mass society requires strict administration and management, making humanity seem undependable and dangerous under such discipline.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: The immense edifice of teacher instruction and schooling in general rests on the shaky hypothesis that expert intervention in childhood produces better people than might otherwise occur. I’ve come to doubt that. A gigantic social investment rides on this hypothesis, one that might otherwise be spent on reducing stress on family life which interferes with happiness and the growth of intelligence.
narration: The vast structure of teacher training and schooling is built on the uncertain belief that expert intervention in childhood creates better individuals than would naturally develop. I've started to question this belief. A significant amount of social resources is tied up in this idea, resources that could instead alleviate family stress, which hampers happiness and the development of intelligence.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: School had become an employment project, the largest hiring hall in the world, bigger than agriculture, bigger than armies.
narration: Schools have transformed into an employment project, the largest hiring hall globally, surpassing even agriculture and armies.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: What the vast industry of professional child-rearing has told you about the right way to grow up matters less than you’ve been led to believe.
narration: The extensive industry of professional child-rearing has convinced you that there's a single correct way to grow up, but that matters less than you've been led to think.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: The net effect of holding children in confinement for twelve years without honor paid to the spirit is a compelling demonstration that the State considers the Western spiritual tradition dangerous, subversive. And of course it is. School is about creating loyalty to certain goals and habits, a vision of life, support for a class structure, an intricate system of human relationships cleverly designed to manufacture the continuous low level of discontent upon which mass production and finance rely.
narration: Keeping children confined for twelve years without acknowledging their spirit shows that the State views the Western spiritual tradition as dangerous. Schooling is designed to foster loyalty to specific goals and habits, supporting a class structure and a system of relationships that maintain a constant level of discontent necessary for mass production and finance.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: Education is the modern world’s temporal religion.
narration: Education has become the modern world's secular religion.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: Spiritually contented people are dangerous for a variety of reasons. They don’t make reliable servants because they won’t jump at every command.
narration: People who are spiritually content can be seen as dangerous; they don't blindly follow commands.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: The transformation businessmen wrought in the idea of education at the end of the nineteenth century and the early decades of the twentieth is the familiar system we have today.
narration: The changes that businessmen made to the concept of education in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries have shaped the familiar system we know today.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: All these incremental changes are ambitious designs to control how children think, feel, and behave. The old-fashioned was to be discarded. Indeed, the study of history itself was stopped. Respect for tradition was held sentimental and counterproductive. Only one thing could not be challenged, and that was the school religion itself, where even minor rebellion was dealt with harshly.
narration: These incremental changes aim to control how children think, feel, and behave. The old ways were to be discarded, and even the study of history was halted. Respect for tradition was seen as sentimental and counterproductive, with only the school religion remaining unchallenged, where even minor rebellion faced harsh consequences.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: Children resist teaching, as they should, but nobody resists learning.
narration: Children may resist teaching, but they do not resist learning.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: Our present system of schooling alienates us so sharply from inner genius, most of us are barred from ever being able to hear our calling. Calling in most of us shrivels to fantasy and daydreams as a remnant of what might have been.
narration: The current schooling system alienates us from our inner genius, preventing many from hearing their true calling. For most, that calling shrivels into mere fantasy and daydreams, remnants of what could have been.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: We subject them to the filthiest, most pornographic regimens of constant surveillance and ranking so they never experience the solitude and reflection necessary to become a whole man or woman.
narration: Students are subjected to constant surveillance and ranking, depriving them of the solitude and reflection needed to become whole individuals.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: The school institution has always had a strong shadow mission to refute the irrefutable fact that all kids want to learn to be their best and strongest selves. They don’t need to be forced to do this. School is a tour de force designed to recreate human nature around a different premise, constructing proof that most kids don’t want to learn because they are biologically defective.
narration: The school system has a hidden agenda that contradicts the undeniable truth: all children naturally want to learn and grow. They shouldn't have to be coerced into this process. Instead, schools are structured to reshape human nature, falsely suggesting that most kids lack the desire to learn due to some inherent flaw.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: School wreaks havoc on human foundations in at least eight substantive ways so deeply buried few notice them.
narration: Schools fundamentally disrupt the very foundations of human development in ways that are often overlooked.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: The first lesson schools teach is forgetfulness; forcing children to forget how they taught themselves important things like walking and talking.
narration: The first lesson of school is forgetfulness, pushing children to abandon the self-taught skills of walking and talking.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: By addicting children to outside approval and nonsense rewards, schools make them indifferent to the real power and potential that inheres in self-discovery reveals.
narration: Schools create a dependency on external validation, dulling children's awareness of their own potential for self-discovery.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: Self-respect in children must be made contingent on the certification of experts through rituals of number magic. It must not be self-generated as it was for Benjamin Franklin, the Wright brothers, Thomas Edison, or Henry Ford.
narration: Children's self-esteem is conditioned by the approval of experts, rather than being cultivated from within, as it was for great innovators like Benjamin Franklin and Thomas Edison.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: School has been built to serve a society of associations: corporations, institutions, and agencies. Kids know this instinctively. How should they feel about it? How should we?
narration: Schools are designed to serve a society of corporations and institutions, and children are aware of this on some level. How should they feel about it? And how should we?

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: School started to destroy my family by dividing us from one another instead of joining us.
narration: School began to tear my family apart instead of bringing us together.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: I was tired of seeing my once happy child constantly in tears.
narration: I grew weary of watching my once joyful child in tears.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: The only conceivable answer is to break the power of these things, through grit, courage, indomitability and resolution if possible, through acts of personal sabotage and disloyalty if not.
narration: The only solution is to dismantle this power through grit and courage, or even through acts of personal sabotage if necessary.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: I defined myself as a child by my accomplishments at school just as I had been taught to.
narration: As a child, I measured my worth by my school achievements, just as I was taught.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: The net effect was to nearly eradicate the intellectual possibilities of school instruction. Did Conant understand the catastrophe he helped induce? I think he did. He would dispute my judgment, of course, that it was a catastrophe.
narration: The overall impact was to almost eliminate the intellectual potential of school instruction. Did Conant realize the disaster he contributed to? I believe he did, though he would likely argue against my view that it was a disaster.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: If you let people learn too much, they might kill you.
narration: If you allow people to learn too much, they might turn against you.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: What can anyone do with human flotsam in a crowded world that scorns their labor and scorns their companionship? Set them to watching television?
narration: What can we do with people who are discarded in a world that disregards their work and their companionship? Perhaps just set them in front of a television.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: The guiding protection is a mechanism to ensure these proletariats don’t learn much lest they become 'demoniac.'
narration: The guiding force is a system designed to keep these working-class individuals from learning too much, for fear they might become dangerous.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: It is our own leadership which lives dangerously, dwelling in a Darwinian world in which its own people are suspect, their danger so far contained by ensnaring the managed population through schooling into a conspiracy against itself. The first Enlightenment was a false one. It merely transferred the right to direct our lives from a corporate Church and a hereditary nobility to a pack of experts whose minds were (and are) for sale to anyone with a checkbook. In the second Enlightenment we need to correct our mistakes, using what schools we decide upon to help us strive for full consciousness, for self-assertion, mental independence, and personal sovereignty—for a release from tutelage for everybody.
narration: Our leadership lives dangerously in a world where its own people are seen as suspect, contained through schooling in a conspiracy against themselves. The first Enlightenment simply shifted control from a corporate Church and hereditary nobility to a group of experts for sale to the highest bidder. In our second Enlightenment, we must correct these mistakes, using schools to promote full consciousness, self-assertion, mental independence, and personal sovereignty for everyone.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: Each year the child is coming to belong more to the State and less and less to the parent.
narration: Every year, children are becoming more the property of the State and less the responsibility of their parents.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: At the heart of the durability of mass schooling is a brilliantly designed power fragmentation system which distributes decision-making so widely among so many different warring interests that large-scale change is impossible to those without a codebook.
narration: The strength of mass schooling lies in a power fragmentation system that spreads decision-making across many conflicting interests, making large-scale change impossible for those without the right knowledge.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: The problem of American education from a personal or a family perspective isn’t really a problem at all from the vantage point of big business, big finance, and big government. What’s a problem to me is a solution for them. An insufficient incentive exists to change things much, otherwise things would change. I learned that from Adam Smith, Smith turns out to be a much different sensibility than the priesthood of corporate apologists thinks he is.
narration: From a personal or family perspective, the issues in American education aren't really problems for big business, finance, or government. What troubles us is often a solution for them. There's not enough incentive to change the system, as I learned from Adam Smith, who offers a different perspective than what corporate apologists suggest.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: For all these and other reasons, the form of schooling we get is largely a kind of consumer and employee training. This isn’t just incidentally true. Common sense should tell you it’s necessarily so if the economy is to survive in any recognizable form.
narration: For many reasons, the schooling we receive primarily serves as training for consumers and employees. This isn't just incidental; it's essential for the economy to maintain its current form.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: School is a place where a comprehensive social vision is learned.
narration: School teaches us a comprehensive social vision.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: The familiar government school curriculum represents enshrined mudsill theory telling us people would do nothing if they weren’t tricked, bribed, or intimidated.
narration: The standard government school curriculum embodies the mudsill theory, suggesting that people need to be tricked, bribed, or intimidated to take action.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: But how on earth can you believe these things in the face of a century of institution-shaping/economy-shaping monopoly schooling which claims something different? ... I hope that makes you think a little. Amish success isn’t even possible according to mudsill theory. They couldn’t have happened and yet they did.
narration: How can you hold onto these beliefs when faced with a century of schooling that shapes institutions and economies differently? Consider the Amish, who defy the odds and thrive without the conventional schooling system.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: The power of elites to set the agenda for public schooling has to be challenged.
narration: We must challenge the power of elites who dictate the agenda for public education.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: We need to smash the government monopoly over the upbringing of our young by forcing it to compete for funds whose commitments should rest largely on the judgment of parents and local associations.
narration: It's time to dismantle the government's monopoly on our children's upbringing and let parents and local communities decide.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: The fundamental, all-pervasive cause of world instability is the destruction of communities by the commercialization of all human relationships and the resulting neuroses and psychoses.
narration: World instability stems from the destruction of communities, driven by the commercialization of human relationships, leading to widespread neuroses and psychoses.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: Here is the crux of the dilemma: modern schooling has no lasting value to exchange for the spectacular chunk of living time it wastes or the possibilities it destroys. The kids know it, their parents know it, you know it, I know it, and the folks who administer the medicine know it. School is a fool’s bargain, we are fools for accepting its dry beans in exchange for our children.
narration: The crux of the dilemma is that modern schooling offers no lasting value for the significant amount of time it consumes or the opportunities it squanders. Everyone knows this—students, parents, and even those who run the system. Accepting this bargain is foolish; we trade our children's potential for something worthless.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: Schools only follow orders.
narration: Schools simply follow the directives they are given.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: If centralizers prevail, the connection between schooling and work will become total; if decentralizers prevail it will be diffuse, irregular, and for many kinds of work, as utterly insignificant as it should be.
narration: If those who centralize succeed, schooling will be completely tied to work. However, if decentralizers win, that connection will be loose and varied, often insignificant, which is how it should be.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: The relentless rationalization of the school world has left the modern student a prisoner of low-grade vocational activities. He lives in a disenchanted world without meaning. Our cultural dilemma here in the United States has little to do with children who don’t read, but lies instead in finding a way to restore meaning and purpose to modern life.
narration: The relentless rationalization of schools has turned students into prisoners of low-grade vocational activities, leaving them in a disenchanted world devoid of meaning. Our cultural dilemma in the United States isn't just about children not reading; it's about finding a way to restore meaning and purpose to modern life.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: Schools thus consume most of the people they incarcerate. School curricula are like unwholesome economies. They don’t deal in basic industries of mind, but instead try to be 'popular,' dealing in the light stuff in an effort to hold down rebellion.
narration: Schools consume most of the people they incarcerate. Their curricula resemble unwholesome economies, avoiding the basic industries of the mind and instead focusing on 'popular' content to suppress rebellion.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: The premise upon which mass compulsion schooling is based is dead wrong. It tries to shoehorn every style, culture, and personality into one ugly boot that fits nobody.
narration: The premise of mass compulsory schooling is fundamentally flawed, attempting to force every style, culture, and personality into a single, ill-fitting mold.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: Standardized tests, like schools themselves, have lost their moral legitimacy.
narration: Standardized tests, much like the schools themselves, have lost their moral legitimacy.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: If educational institutions are so demonstrably successful, why are we always hearing about their failures? ... The reforms are therefore never completed. To do so would require admitting failure, or worse, admitting that the failure is not failure at all, only a continuing round in the socialized enforcement of intellectual and emotional dependency.
narration: If educational institutions are truly successful, why do we constantly hear about their failures? The reforms never seem to end, as admitting failure would mean acknowledging that the issue isn't failure at all, but rather a continuous cycle of enforcing intellectual and emotional dependency.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Gatto views school as a conflict pitting the needs of social machinery against those of the human spirit.
narration: Gatto sees school as a struggle between the demands of social machinery and the needs of the human spirit.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: What the Combine needs, most of all, is Wal-Mart clerks and burger flippers and dedicated but low-paid, government-employed 'foreign service officers' proud of their titles as teachers, who prevent the restless natives from rebelling while the extraction of resources and capital, human and otherwise, continues unabated.
narration: What the Combine truly seeks are Wal-Mart clerks, burger flippers, and low-paid teachers who keep the restless masses from rebelling while resources and capital are extracted without pause.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: I began to realize that the bells and the confinement, the crazy sequences, the age-segregation, the lack of privacy, the constant surveillance, and all the rest of the national curriculum of schooling were designed exactly as if someone had set out to prevent children from learning how to think and act.
narration: I started to see that the bells, confinement, and constant surveillance in schools seemed designed to stop children from learning how to think and act.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: The lesson of numbered classes is that everyone has a proper place in the pyramid and that there is no way out of your class except by number magic. Failing that, you must stay where you are put.
narration: The lesson of numbered classes teaches that everyone has a designated place in the hierarchy, and escaping that class is nearly impossible. If you can't break free, you simply remain where you are.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: School takes our children away from any possibility of an active role in community life — in fact, it destroys communities by relegating the training of children to the hands of certified experts — and by doing so it ensures our children cannot grow up fully human.
narration: School removes children from any chance of engaging actively in their communities, effectively dismantling those communities by placing child education in the hands of certified experts, which ultimately hinders their development as fully human beings.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: The current debate about whether we should have a national curriculum is phony. We already have a national curriculum locked up in the seven lessons I have just outlined.
narration: The ongoing debate about a national curriculum is insincere; we already have one embedded in the seven lessons I've described.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: School is a twelve-year jail sentence where bad habits are the only curriculum truly learned. I teach school and win awards doing it. I should know.
narration: School feels like a twelve-year jail sentence where the only real lessons learned are bad habits. I teach in this system and even win awards, so I know what I'm talking about.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: It is absurd and anti-life to be part of a system that compels you to sit in confinement with people of exactly the same age and social class.
narration: It's absurd and anti-life to be forced into a system where you're confined with only people of the same age and social class.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: The truth is that schools don’t really teach anything except how to obey orders.
narration: The reality is that schools primarily teach one thing: how to obey orders.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: All the pathologies we’ve considered come about in large measure because the lessons of school prevent children from keeping important appointments with themselves and with their families to learn lessons in self-motivation, perseverance, self-reliance, courage, dignity, and love.
narration: Many of the issues we've discussed arise largely because school lessons keep children from important personal and family commitments, preventing them from learning vital lessons in self-motivation, perseverance, and love.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Right now we are taking from our children all the time that they need to develop self-knowledge. That has to stop. We have to invent school experiences that give a lot of that time back. We need to trust children from a very early age with independent study, perhaps arranged in school, but which takes place away from the institutional setting.
narration: Currently, we're robbing our children of the time they need to develop self-knowledge. This must change. We need to create school experiences that allow them to reclaim that time, trusting them with independent study, even if it's organized within the school but occurs outside the institutional environment.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: If we use schooling to break children away from parents — and make no mistake, that has been the central function of schools since John Cotton announced it as the purpose of the Bay Colony schools in 1650 and Horace Mann announced it as the purpose of Massachusetts schools in 1850 — we’re going to continue to have the horror show we have right now.
narration: If we continue to use schooling as a means to separate children from their parents — a practice that has been the core function of schools since the 17th century — we will perpetuate the current nightmare.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: We don’t need more schooling — we need less. Compulsory schooling in factory schools is a very recent, very Massachusetts/New York development. It is a fact generally ignored when considering the communal nature of institutional families like schools, large corporations, colleges, armies, hospitals, and government agencies that they are not real communities at all, but networks. By preempting fifty percent of the total time of the young, by locking young people up with other young people exactly their own age, by ringing bells to start and stop work, by asking people to think about the same thing at the same time in the same way, by grading people the way we grade vegetables — and in a dozen other vile and stupid ways — network schools steal the vitality of communities and replace it with an ugly mechanism.
narration: We don't need more schooling; we need less. Compulsory schooling in factory schools is a recent development. It's often overlooked that institutional families like schools and corporations aren't real communities, but networks. By taking away half of young people's time, locking them with peers, ringing bells to control their schedules, and grading them like vegetables, network schools rob communities of their vitality and replace it with a harsh mechanism.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Networks make people lonely. They cannot correct their inhuman mechanism and still succeed as networks.
narration: Networks create loneliness, and they can't fix their inhuman nature while still functioning as networks.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: This business we call 'education' — when we mean 'schooling' — makes an interesting example of network values in conflict with traditional community values. For one hundred and fifty years institutional education has seen fit to offer as its main purpose the preparation for economic success. Good education = good job, good money, good things. This has become the universal national banner, hoisted by Harvards as well as high schools.
narration: The concept of education, which we often equate with schooling, illustrates a clash between network values and traditional community values. For over a century, institutional education has primarily aimed to prepare individuals for economic success, promoting the idea that good education leads to good jobs and financial prosperity. This notion has become a widespread mantra, embraced by prestigious universities and high schools alike.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: The absurdity of defining education as an economic good becomes clear if we ask ourselves what is gained by perceiving education as a way to enhance even further the runaway consumption that threatens the earth, the air, and the water of our planet? Should we continue to teach people that they can buy happiness in the face of a tidal wave of evidence that they cannot?
narration: It's absurd to define education merely as an economic commodity when we consider what is truly gained by viewing it as a means to fuel rampant consumption that endangers our planet. Should we persist in teaching that happiness can be purchased, despite overwhelming evidence to the contrary?

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Mass education cannot work to produce a fair society because its daily practice is practice in rigged competition, suppression, and intimidation. The schools we’ve allowed to develop can’t work to teach nonmaterial values, the values which give meaning to everyone’s life, rich or poor, because the structure of schooling is held together by a Byzantine tapestry of reward and threat, of carrots and sticks.
narration: Mass education fails to create a fair society, as it perpetuates rigged competition, suppression, and intimidation. The schools we've established cannot impart the nonmaterial values that enrich lives, regardless of wealth, because their very structure relies on a complex web of rewards and punishments.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Official favor, grades, or other trinkets of subordination have no connection with education; they are the paraphernalia of servitude, not of freedom.
narration: Grades and official recognition are not linked to true education; instead, they represent tools of subordination rather than instruments of freedom.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Schools stifle family originality by appropriating the critical time needed for any sound idea of family to develop — then they blame the family for its failure to be a family.
narration: Schools suppress the unique qualities of families by taking away essential time needed for genuine family development, only to later blame families for not functioning effectively.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Government monopoly schools use a different blueprint these days, of course. People are drawn willy-nilly out of large 'catchment' areas and dumped together into compartments according to similar scores on standardized tests. There they are exhorted to perform and behave according to the specifications of strangers.
narration: Today, government monopoly schools follow a different blueprint. Students are randomly grouped from large areas and placed together based on standardized test scores, where they are pressured to conform to the expectations of strangers.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: By preventing a free market in education, a handful of social engineers, backed by the industries that profit from compulsory schooling — teacher colleges, textbook publishers, materials suppliers, and others — has ensured that most of our children will not have an education, even though they may be thoroughly schooled.
narration: By blocking a free market in education, a small group of social engineers, supported by industries profiting from compulsory schooling, has guaranteed that many children will not receive a true education, despite being thoroughly schooled.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: The best immediate evidence I have to offer, that leaving people alone to work out their own local destinies is a splendid idea, is the curious sociology of my presence as a speaker in Dedham last year.
narration: A strong example of the benefits of allowing people to determine their own local destinies is my experience as a speaker in Dedham last year.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Only slaves are gathered by others.
narration: Only slaves are forced together by others.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: American education teaches by its methodology that people are machines. Bells ring, circuits open and close, energy flows or is constricted, qualities are reduced to a numbering system, a plan is followed of which the machine parts know nothing.
narration: The way American education operates suggests that people are treated like machines. With bells ringing and systems in place, everything is reduced to numbers, and the plan is followed without the knowledge of those involved.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: This system doesn’t work, and it’s one of the causes of our world coming apart. No amount of tinkering will make the school machine work to produce educated people; education and schooling are, as we all have experienced, mutually exclusive terms.
narration: This system is failing and is contributing to the disintegration of our world. No amount of adjustments will make the school system effective in producing educated individuals; education and schooling are fundamentally different.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: We have destroyed the minds and characters of the nation’s children by preempting their youth, removing their choices.
narration: We've harmed the minds and characters of our children by taking away their youth and their choices.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: It is illegitimate to have an expert answer that question for you.
narration: It's not right to let an expert dictate the answer to that question for you.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: He questioned the validity and relevance of standardized tests, the prison atmosphere of school, and the lack of relevant experience given students. Unfortunately, our school did not allow that dialogue to happen, except for a few teachers who had the courage to engage the students. What was not reported in the newspaper is the fact that the school authorities called the police to intervene and ‘restore the peace’ which, ironically enough, was never in the slightest jeopardy as the student audience was well-behaved and attentive throughout.
narration: Gatto raised important questions about standardized tests, the prison-like atmosphere of schools, and the lack of relevant experiences for students. Sadly, the school stifled the necessary dialogue, with only a few brave teachers willing to engage. Interestingly, authorities even called the police to maintain order, despite the students being attentive and well-behaved.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Forced schooling seduces the trapped into believing that inert knowledge, memorized fact bits and sequences is the gold standard of intellectual achievement. Learning to connect those bits into meanings for yourself is discouraged. This habit training is a major weapon of mass instruction.
narration: Compulsory schooling tricks students into thinking that memorizing facts is the pinnacle of learning. In reality, it discourages them from making connections and understanding those facts, turning education into a mere habit of rote instruction.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: What if there is no 'problem' with our schools? What if they are the way they are, so expensively flying in the face of common sense and long experience in how children learn things, not because they are doing something wrong, but because they are doing something right?
narration: What if our schools aren't failing but are actually succeeding in their design? What if they are functioning exactly as intended, despite contradicting common sense about how children learn?

author: John Taylor Gatto
