text: The whole diseased empire of institutional schooling is, like the wonderful one-horse shay, extremely fragile.
narration: The entire system of institutional schooling is surprisingly delicate, much like a fragile structure.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: The obligation to amuse and instruct myself was entirely my own, and people who didn’t know that were childish people, to be avoided if possible.
narration: It was my responsibility to find joy and learning on my own, and those who couldn't do that were to be seen as immature.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: School trains children to be employees and consumers; teach your own to be leaders and adventurers. School trains children to obey reflexively; teach your own to think critically and independently. Well-schooled kids have a low threshold for boredom; help your own to develop an inner life so that they’ll never be bored.
narration: Schools condition children to become obedient employees and consumers. Instead, encourage your children to be leaders and adventurers. While well-schooled kids may struggle with boredom, help your children cultivate an inner life that keeps them engaged.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Mandatory education serves children only incidentally; its real purpose is to turn them into servants.
narration: Compulsory education is designed not to serve children, but to transform them into obedient servants.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: We have become a nation of children, happy to surrender our judgments and our wills to political exhortations and commercial blandishments that would insult actual adults.
narration: We've turned into a society of children, willingly giving up our judgment and will to political and commercial pressures that would offend true adults.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: The terrifying drop in literacy between the end of WWII and the beginning of the Korean War happened inside a single decade. Even the most fervent natural selection enthusiast wouldn’t argue things work that quickly. The Bell Curve held that violence in black society was genetically programmed; but once again, data from outside the charmed circle in America pushing this biological reality was contradictory.
narration: The alarming decline in literacy from the end of World War II to the start of the Korean War occurred in just a decade. Even the staunchest proponents of natural selection wouldn't claim such rapid changes. The Bell Curve suggested that violence in black society was genetically determined, yet data from outside the U.S. contradicts this notion.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: School should develop the power to withdraw from the external world.
narration: The purpose of school, according to Harris, is to cultivate the ability to detach from the outside world.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: The transformation of school from a place of modest ambitions centering around reading, writing, arithmetic, and decency into a behavioral training laboratory ordered up by 'certain industrialists and the innovative who were altering the nature of the industrial process' has acted to poison the American experiment.
narration: The shift of schools from nurturing basic skills like reading, writing, and arithmetic to becoming behavioral training centers, driven by industrialists, has corrupted the essence of the American experiment.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: In the new world of forced schooling, the training of the young was simply too important a matter to be left to pedagogues, just as had been the situation in ancient Rome. The real question you should be asking is why, in all the years of school incarceration you suffered, did nobody bother to let you in on the secret?
narration: In today's world of compulsory schooling, the education of our youth is deemed too critical to be entrusted to educators alone, much like in ancient Rome. The pressing question is, why was the truth about your years of confinement in school never revealed to you?

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Forced schooling represents a big step backwards from the exciting free market in learning offered by the bazaar of American life.
narration: Compulsory schooling marks a significant regression from the vibrant marketplace of learning that American life once provided.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Under the new system, the goals of good moral values, good citizenship skills, and good personal development were exchanged for a novel fourth purpose — becoming a human resource to be spent by businessmen and politicians.
narration: In this new educational framework, the focus shifted from fostering moral values and personal growth to a troubling new objective: transforming students into mere resources for business and political agendas.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: The ultimate goal implanted in student minds, which replaced the earlier goal of independent livelihoods, was getting a good job.
narration: The new aim instilled in students shifted from achieving independence to simply securing a good job.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: As soon as you understand the functions it was given to perform in the new corporate economy, nothing about school at all should surprise you.
narration: Once you grasp the roles assigned to schools within the corporate landscape, everything about the educational system begins to make sense.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: School had to train in consumption habits: listening to others, moving on a bell or horn signal without questioning, becoming impressionable — more accurately, gullible — in order to do well on tests. Kids who insisted on producing their own lives had to be humiliated publicly as a warning to others. Producing yourself is the ultimate form of production.
narration: Schools are designed to instill consumption habits, training children to listen and respond without question, becoming impressionable for the sake of passing tests. Those who dare to create their own paths face public humiliation, serving as a warning to others. The true essence of production lies in self-creation.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Universal schooling had weakened the imagination, just as Spinoza predicted it would in 1690, and Fichte predicted in the second decade of the 19th century.
narration: Universal schooling has stifled imagination, just as Spinoza and Fichte foresaw centuries ago.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: The most reckless failure of our modern American nation is how we waste people in the interest of keeping systems efficient. Systems won’t protect us in the time of the sweat bath we’ve entered; systems can’t heal the diseased economy which systems-thinkers have built — only sovereign human spirits intact can do that, the kind which can’t be fashioned in factory schools.
narration: One of the greatest failures of modern America is the way we squander human potential for the sake of system efficiency. These systems cannot save us; only resilient, independent spirits can, and those cannot be created in factory-like schools.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Schooling demanded separation of people from daily life, it divided the world into disciplines, courses, classes, grades and teachers who would remain strangers to the children in all but name. Even religion, separated from family and daily life, was just another subject for critical analysis and testing. And the constant competition was destructive, leaving a multitude of losers, humiliated and self-hating, a far cry from the universal commitment Amish community life requires.
narration: Schooling required a separation from daily life, breaking the world into disciplines, courses, classes, and grades, with teachers who were often strangers to the children. Even religion became just another subject for analysis and testing, while constant competition led to many feeling like losers, humiliated and self-hating, which starkly contrasts with the commitment to community life valued by the Amish.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: We need to abandon the notion — and punish those who retain it — that ordinary people are too stupid, irresponsible, and childish to look out for themselves.
narration: We must reject the idea that ordinary people are too foolish or irresponsible to take care of themselves.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: You can’t self-teach without inner strength and a measure of gravity, without opportunities to be alone, to have broad experience with people and great challenges. Most of us who presume to judge schools are fooled by rituals of disciplined behavior, pretty hall displays, and test scores.
narration: Self-teaching requires inner strength and opportunities for solitude, as well as diverse experiences with people and challenges. Many who judge schools are misled by the rituals of discipline, attractive displays, and test scores.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: The degree of disrespect our nation has assigned its ordinary population wouldn’t be possible unless somewhere in the command centers it hadn’t been decided that common men and women should be stripped of any power to rebel.
narration: The level of disrespect shown to ordinary people indicates a deliberate decision to strip them of any power to resist.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: School as Narcotic What have we done, Murray? Filling blackboards and workbooks, running videos, cramming heads with disconnected information we have driven even the idea of quality from the field. And by constantly bathing the young in passivity, showering them with petty orders and bells for their own good, we have created a foundry where incomplete men and women are forged.
narration: What have we done? By filling blackboards and workbooks, running videos, and cramming heads with disconnected information, we have driven the idea of quality from education. Constantly bathing the young in passivity and showering them with petty orders has created a foundry where incomplete individuals are forged.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Government schooling is the most radical adventure in history. It kills the family by monopolizing the best times of childhood and by teaching disrespect for home and parents.
narration: Government schooling is a radical adventure that undermines families by monopolizing childhood and teaching disrespect for home and parents.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Ordinary people send their children to school to get smart, but what modern schooling teaches is dumbness. Old-fashioned dumbness used to be simple ignorance. Now it’s been transformed into permanent mathematical categories of relative stupidity, such as 'gifted and talented,' 'mainstream,' and 'special ed.'
narration: Parents send their kids to school to get smart, yet modern schooling teaches them dumbness. What was once simple ignorance has now become permanent categories of relative stupidity, like 'gifted and talented' or 'special ed.'

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: The institution you and I work for creates addiction. It addicts children to prefer thin abstraction and dull fantasies to reality.
narration: The institution we work for creates addiction, leading children to prefer thin abstractions and dull fantasies over reality.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: In a system more congenial to learning (and less to social control) the thrill of doing the labor would more than outweigh the effort required. And, of course, if everyone in the society were on the same page about the necessity of developing intellect and character in the young (not weighting them down with chains), the work would be. . . child’s play.
narration: In a system that truly supports learning rather than social control, the joy of engaging in meaningful work would far surpass the effort needed. If society collectively recognized the importance of nurturing intellect and character in our youth, free from burdens, the process would be as easy as child's play.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: School is not a good place for your kids.
narration: School isn't a healthy environment for your children.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Once a profile was created, the second step was to add a personalized Wishes and Weaknesses component. I asked each student to list three things each wanted to be knowledgeable about by the end of the year — that was the wishes part — and three weaknesses he or she wished to overcome, deficiencies which led to humiliation or failures of opportunity.
narration: After creating a profile, the next step involved adding a personalized component for wishes and weaknesses. I encouraged each student to identify three areas they wanted to learn about by year's end and three weaknesses they aimed to overcome, which often stemmed from feelings of humiliation or missed opportunities.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: We become what we behold. It’s something to remember, Columbine.
narration: We become what we observe. It's a crucial reminder, especially in light of events like Columbine.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: “Proper” schooling teaches “negatively,” it never allows the working classes or the poor “to read sufficiently well to understand what they do read.” Playfair argued that public instruction would ruin national prosperity, not enhance it. And who is to say he is wrong as long as prosperity is reckoned in dollars and cents.
narration: Proper schooling teaches negatively, preventing the working classes and the poor from reading well enough to understand what they read. Playfair argued that public instruction would ruin national prosperity, not enhance it. And who can say he is wrong as long as prosperity is measured in dollars and cents?

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: It’s not so much kids think in these abstract terms, it’s the widespread understanding among the young that school isn’t about them (and their interests, curiosities and futures), but exclusively about the wishes of other people. School is built around the self-interest of others.
narration: It's not that kids think in abstract terms; it's the widespread understanding that school isn't about them or their interests, curiosities, and futures, but rather about the wishes of others. School is constructed around the self-interest of others.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: As long as school is the delivery vehicle, any undertaking is held in contempt.
narration: As long as school serves as the delivery vehicle, any effort is met with contempt.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: The simple truth is that Australian schools will never be allowed to study and debate vital matters, even though they are sentenced to inherit the mess.
narration: The simple truth is that Australian schools will never be permitted to study and debate crucial issues, even though they are destined to inherit the consequences.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: You can’t win liberty by memorizing what you’re told to memorize. The watchword of school is 'Let others think for you.' Kids don’t understand why it’s happening, but they sense themselves being put to death in school. That’s why they hate it so much.
narration: You can't achieve liberty by simply memorizing what you're told. In school, the motto is 'Let others think for you.' Children may not fully grasp it, but they feel as though they're being stifled in school, which is why they often despise it.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Learning isn’t the point of school, winning is.
narration: In school, the focus isn't on learning; it's all about winning.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: The educated mind is the connected mind, connected to all manner of different human styles... connected to a dizzying profusion of intellectual ideas which interconnect with one another, and in time, set the pulses racing with the sheer transcendence possible in the human prospect.
narration: An educated mind is one that connects with diverse human experiences and a wealth of intellectual ideas, creating a vibrant tapestry that inspires and elevates the human spirit.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: The project started in earnest at the beginning of the century and was acknowledged and even boasted about — by the dean of Teacher Education at Stanford University, who played a hand in its inception. As a weapon of mass instruction it’s superior in its destructive effect to all the others, the master weapon as it were. It’s a principal cause of the intense and growing childishness of Americans in every social class, an indictment I hear from every corner of the world as I travel — and increasingly from domestic commentators, too.
narration: The project began in earnest at the start of the century, proudly acknowledged by the dean of Teacher Education at Stanford University. This weapon of mass instruction has a destructive effect that surpasses all others, causing a growing childishness among Americans across all social classes, a concern echoed by observers worldwide.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: The dark side of the Welfare State midwifed by Beatrice Webb and the Fabian socialists is not its superficial purpose of being kind, but its intention of killing with kindness, and thus protecting the interests of the better people, non-violently. The talking choo-choo is the tip of the tip of the iceberg on which the damned are consigned to be frozen intellectually, psychologically, and socially.
narration: The dark side of the Welfare State, shaped by figures like Beatrice Webb and the Fabian socialists, isn't just about kindness; it's about a subtle form of control that protects the interests of the privileged. The talking choo-choo represents just the tip of an iceberg, under which many are trapped, frozen in their intellectual and social development.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: The formula for talking choo-choo social control is set down in Calvin’s Institutes (1535), refined impressively in Spinoza’s Tractate (1670), and brought to institutional life by Fichte in the second decade of the 19th century. Since then it’s been off to the races.
narration: The formula for this form of social control can be traced back to Calvin’s Institutes and Spinoza’s Tractate, later institutionalized by Fichte in the early 19th century. Since then, it has proliferated widely.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: The effect of these amoral exercises is severe on children, despite apologetics practiced by child development experts who justify the practice. There are talking animals in Potter, but none are cute. 'Thank goodness my education was neglected,' Potter wrote, but what she meant was talking choo-choo schooling.
narration: The impact of these amoral exercises on children is profound, even as child development experts defend them. Potter's tales feature talking animals, but none are cute. She expressed gratitude for her neglected education, referring specifically to the kind of schooling that stifles true learning.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: If you don’t see this, you’re doing something seriously wrong. Even at seven don’t edit the truth out of things. If the family has an income kids need to know to the penny what it is and how it’s spent.
narration: If you don't recognize this, something is seriously amiss. Even at the age of seven, don't shield them from the truth. If the family has an income, children should understand exactly what it is and how it's allocated.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Schools are the principal factory in which that is done; consumption on the other hand has to be enhanced — and no condition stimulates consumption like boredom, especially when the imagination and the inner life have been paralyzed by endless memory drills, the synthetic crises of continual testing, and a thorough conditioning in rewards and punishments, the game of winners and losers.
narration: Schools serve as the main factory for this process, where boredom is a key driver of consumption, especially when imagination and inner life are stifled by endless memory drills, constant testing, and a strict system of rewards and punishments, creating a game of winners and losers.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: The destruction of uninterrupted time is a major weapon of mass instruction. Schools are a rat’s maze of frantic activity: bells, loudspeakers, messengers pounding on classroom doors, shrieks from the playground, official visitors, unofficial visitors, toilet interruptions coming and going, catcalls, bullyings and flirtings — you never know when the next interruption will appear.
narration: The elimination of uninterrupted time is a powerful tool of mass instruction. Schools resemble a chaotic maze filled with constant distractions: bells, loudspeakers, messengers, playground noise, and various interruptions, making it impossible to focus.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: The principal target of school time at present, a target many self-satisfied men and women congratulate themselves upon knowing, is the production of high standardized test scores — which correlate with almost nothing of value.
narration: Currently, the main focus of school time is on producing high standardized test scores, which actually correlate with very little of real value.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: High standards and standardization are two very different things but you have been deliberately led by the rules of Newspeak to regard them as the same, just as you’ve been conditioned to think of education and schooling in the same breath.
narration: High standards and standardization are fundamentally different, yet you've been misled to see them as identical, just as you've been conditioned to equate education with schooling.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Any type of change which will produce new value for our society through schooling will involve less school time, less school personnel, less store-bought materials, less interference in the natural processes of learning.
narration: To create real value in our society through education, we need to reduce school time, personnel, and reliance on commercial materials, allowing for more natural learning processes.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Grades and test scores are a terrible measure of quality. The sooner we recognize that in planning, the sooner we’ll reach better ways to help our young. After teaching for 30 years, and winning awards doing it, I can say flatly what everyone knows in their gut — the best readers, the best writers, the best mathematicians, the best scientists are almost never people who got the best grades.
narration: Grades and test scores really don't reflect true quality. The sooner we understand this, the better we can support our youth. After three decades of teaching and receiving accolades, I can confidently say that the best readers, writers, mathematicians, and scientists rarely come from the top of the grade scale.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: School as Jail; Jail as School. The United States, traditional land of the free, now jails 25% of all prisoners on earth, 90% for non-violent crimes. With 5% of the global population, we are five times more eager than average to lock up our fellow citizens, six times more likely than China is to do the same thing.
narration: Think of school as a jail, and jail as school. The United States, once known as the land of the free, now imprisons a staggering 25% of the world's prisoners, with 90% incarcerated for non-violent offenses. With just 5% of the global population, we are five times more inclined to lock up our citizens than the average, and six times more than China.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Factory schooling replaced our historically libertarian forms of schooling at the beginning of the 20th century in order to standardize a kaleidoscopic pattern of individualized lives which tended to go off in many directions. A managerial utopia could not be made from such raw material; liberty plays no part in managerial efficiency.
narration: Factory schooling took over our traditionally free forms of education in the early 20th century, aiming to standardize the diverse paths of individual lives. A managerial utopia can't be built from such variety; after all, liberty doesn't fit into the framework of managerial efficiency.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: The connection between schooling as you know it, including collegiate schooling, and education is mostly a masterpiece of fabrication. Schooling is organized by command and control from without; education is self-organized from within; school disconnects its clientele from other primary sources of learning. It must do that to achieve administrative efficiency; education sets out to provide a set of bountiful connections which are random, willful, promiscuous, even disharmonious with one another.
narration: The connection between schooling and education is largely a fabrication. Schooling is controlled from the outside, while education is self-directed. Schools cut off students from other sources of learning to maintain efficiency, whereas education fosters diverse and spontaneous connections.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: It’s an obscene form of mental colonization, a reckless waste of your all-too-short existence.
narration: It's a shocking form of mental colonization, wasting your precious life.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: The Dartmouths of the world are the enemies of truth.
narration: Institutions like Dartmouth stand against the pursuit of truth.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Year after year, the International Happiness Survey reports only three conditions necessary to judge your own life a happy one: 1) good relationships 2) good health 3) satisfying work. But school... sets up conditions in which bad health is difficult to escape, relationships are given no time or space to grow in.
narration: The International Happiness Survey shows that happiness relies on good relationships, health, and fulfilling work. Yet, schools create environments that hinder health and relationships.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: In systems logic, concepts like liberty and individual rights are direct attacks on the integrity of systems. Fichte saw that no efficient national management was possible as long as the ordinary population regarded itself as sovereign individuals possessing free will and imagination; therefore he demanded compulsory schooling aimed at removing these things.
narration: In systems logic, ideas like liberty and individual rights threaten the integrity of those systems. Fichte recognized that efficient national management couldn't happen if people saw themselves as sovereign individuals with free will and imagination, which is why he called for compulsory schooling to eliminate those traits.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: The growing incoherence of American society stands upon a foundation of forced schooling in fancy places like Highland, no less than in Harlem, Watts, or East St. Louis.
narration: The increasing incoherence in American society is built on a foundation of forced schooling, whether in affluent areas like Highland or in places like Harlem, Watts, or East St. Louis.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: What reasoning, what philosophy, what values hide behind this action?
narration: What reasoning, what philosophy, what values are concealed behind this action?

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: This is crazy on the face of it.
narration: This is absurd at its core.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Standardized testing is the tail wagging the entire monster of forced institutional schooling. The frequent ceremonies of useless testing — preparation, administration, recovery — convert forced schooling into a travesty of what education should be; they drain hundreds of millions of days yearly from what might otherwise be productive pursuits; they divert tens of billions of cash resources into private pockets.
narration: Standardized testing drives the entire beast of compulsory schooling. The constant rituals of pointless testing — from preparation to administration and recovery — turn forced schooling into a mockery of true education, wasting countless days that could be spent on meaningful activities and siphoning off billions into private hands.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Standardized testing, which has always been about standardization and never about quality standards, must no longer be debated, but brutally and finally destroyed if schooling is ever again to take up a mission of intellect and character enhancement.
narration: Standardized testing has always focused on standardization, not quality. It must be decisively destroyed if schooling is to reclaim its mission of fostering intellect and character.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: The very label 'school' makes a mockery of these rights. We are a worse nation for this radical betrayal visited upon us by generations of political managers masquerading as leaders. And we are a materially poorer nation, as well.
narration: The term 'school' itself mocks our rights. We have become a worse nation due to the betrayal by generations of political managers pretending to be leaders, and we are materially poorer for it.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: The Bartleby Project begins by inviting 60,000,000 American students, one by one, to peacefully refuse to take standardized tests or to participate in any preparation for these tests; it asks them to act because adults chained to institutions and corporations are unable to; because these tests pervert education, are disgracefully inaccurate, impose brutal stresses without reason, and actively encourage a class system which is poisoning the future of the nation. The Bartleby Project should allow no compromise. That will be the second line of defense for management, a standard trick taught in political science seminars. Don’t fall for it. Reject compromise.
narration: The Bartleby Project invites 60 million American students to peacefully refuse standardized tests and any preparation for them. It calls for action because adults tied to institutions can't act; these tests distort education, impose unnecessary stress, and foster a harmful class system. The project must not allow compromise, which is a common tactic used by management.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Just say: I would prefer not to take your test.
narration: Simply say, 'I would prefer not to take your test.'

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Boredom was everywhere in my world, and if you asked the kids, as I often did, why they felt so bored, they always gave the same answers: they said the work was stupid, that it made no sense, that they already knew it. They said they wanted to be doing something real, not just sitting around. They said teachers didn't seem to know much about their subjects and clearly weren't interested in learning more. And the kids were right: their teachers were every bit as bored as they were.
narration: Boredom was everywhere in my world. When I asked the kids why they felt bored, they always gave the same answers: the work was stupid, it made no sense, they already knew it. They wanted to be doing something real, not just sitting around. They said teachers didn't seem to know much about their subjects and weren't interested in learning more. And they were right: their teachers were just as bored.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Is it possible that George W. Bush accidentally spoke the truth when he said we would 'leave no child behind'? Could it be that our schools are designed to make sure not one of them ever really grows up?
narration: Is it possible that George W. Bush accidentally spoke the truth when he said we would 'leave no child behind'? Could it be that our schools are designed to ensure that not one of them ever really grows up?

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Schools are meant to tag the unfit - with poor grades, remedial placement, and other punishments - clearly enough that their peers will accept them as inferior and effectively bar them from the reproductive sweepstakes. That's what all those little humiliations from first grade onward were intended to do: wash the dirt down the drain.
narration: Schools are designed to label the unfit with poor grades and remedial placements, making sure their peers see them as inferior and bar them from opportunities. Those little humiliations from first grade onward were meant to wash the dirt down the drain.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: School trains children to be employees and consumers; teach your own to be leaders and adventurers. School trains children to obey reflexively; teach your own to think critically and independently.
narration: School trains children to be employees and consumers; teach your own to be leaders and adventurers. It conditions them to obey reflexively; teach your own to think critically and independently.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: The new dumbness - the non thought of received ideas - is much more dangerous than simple ignorance, because it's really about thought control. In school, a washing away of the innate power of individual mind takes place, a 'cleansing' so comprehensive that original thinking becomes difficult.
narration: The new dumbness, the absence of independent thought, is far more dangerous than mere ignorance. In schools, there's a systematic erasure of our innate ability to think, a cleansing so thorough that original thought becomes a challenge.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Real knowledge has to be earned by hard and painful thinking; it can't be generated in group discussions or group therapies but only in lonely sessions with yourself.
narration: True knowledge is gained through hard, solitary thinking, not through group discussions or therapies.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Schools that seem to be successful almost always are made to appear so by selective enrollment of self-motivated children.
narration: Schools that appear successful often owe their reputation to the selective enrollment of motivated students.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: The interests are radically different, one an individual thing, the other a collective. You can make your own son or daughter one of a kind if you have the time and will to do so; school can only make them part of a hive, a herd, or an anthill.
narration: The interests of parents and schools are fundamentally different. You can nurture your child to be unique, while school tends to mold them into just another part of the collective.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Compulsory schooling has been, from the beginning, a scheme of indoctrination into the new concept of mass man, an important part of which was the creation of a proletariat. According to Auguste Comte (surely the godfather of scientific schooling), you could create a useful proletariat class by breaking connections between children and their families, their communities, their God, and themselves.
narration: From the very start, compulsory schooling has been a method of indoctrination into the concept of mass man, which included the creation of a proletariat. Auguste Comte, often seen as the father of scientific schooling, believed that breaking the connections between children and their families, communities, and even God was essential to create a useful proletariat.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: The sheer craziness of what we do to our children should have been sufficient cause to stop it once the lunacy was manifest in increased social pathology, but a crucial development forestalled corrective action: schooling became the biggest business of all.
narration: The madness of what we impose on our children should have prompted us to halt it, especially as we witnessed the rise in social issues. Yet, a significant turning point prevented any corrective measures: schooling evolved into the largest business of all.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: When you take the free will and solitude out of education it becomes schooling. You can't have it both ways.
narration: When you remove free will and solitude from education, it transforms into mere schooling. You can't have it both ways.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Real books are deeply subversive of collectivization. They are the best known way to escape herd behavior, because they are vehicles transporting their reader into deep caverns of absolute solitude where nobody else can visit: No two people ever read the same great book.
narration: Real books challenge collectivization. They offer a unique escape from herd mentality, guiding readers into profound solitude where no one else can intrude. Each person experiences a great book in their own way.

author: Jonathan Kozol
text: What is the result? We are preparing a generation of robots. Kids are learning exclusively through rote. We have children who are given no conceptual framework. They do not learn to think, because their teachers are straitjacketed by tests that measure only isolated skills. As a result, they can be given no electives, nothing wonderful or fanciful or beautiful, nothing that touches the spirit or the soul.
narration: The outcome is that we're creating a generation of robots. Kids are learning solely through rote memorization, lacking any conceptual understanding. They aren't taught to think because their teachers are constrained by tests that only assess isolated skills. Consequently, there's no room for electives or anything inspiring that touches the spirit.

author: Jonathan Kozol
text: We see students going off to Rutgers every year. By the end of the first semester they are back in Camden. So we teach them failure. When you think of what their peers in Cherry Hill have gotten in the same years, it seems terribly unfair. I call it failure by design.
narration: Every year, we see students heading off to Rutgers, but by the end of the first semester, they're back in Camden. This cycle teaches them failure. When you compare their experiences to those of their peers in Cherry Hill, it feels incredibly unfair. I call it failure by design.

author: Jonathan Kozol
text: What is the message that it gives to children to grow up surrounded by trash burners, dumpsites and enormous prisons?
narration: What message does it send to children when they grow up surrounded by trash burners, dumpsites, and massive prisons?

author: Jonathan Kozol
text: What we call a ‘C’ at our school is a ‘D’ in Cherry Hill.
narration: At our school, what we consider a 'C' would be a 'D' in Cherry Hill.

author: Jonathan Kozol
text: They have that money goin’ to their schools. They have a nice clean school to go to. They have carpets on the floors and air-conditioned rooms and brand-new books.
narration: They have money flowing into their schools, a clean environment, carpets on the floors, air-conditioned rooms, and brand-new books.

author: Jonathan Kozol
text: We need our education too. Make it equal.
narration: We need our education too. Let's make it equal.

author: Everett Reimer
text: Schools are an essential element of a world in which technology is king. The contradictions of such a world are becoming apparent. They are best illustrated in the school and best corrected by freeing education from the school so that people may learn the truth about the society in which they live.
narration: Schools play a crucial role in a world dominated by technology. The contradictions of this reality are becoming clearer, and the best way to address them is by liberating education from traditional schooling, allowing people to discover the truth about their society.

author: Everett Reimer
text: For most members of the present generation, this hope that their children will benefit more from school than they, is doomed to disappointment. Schools are too expensive for this hope to be realized.
narration: For many in today's generation, the hope that their children will gain more from school than they did is likely to be unfulfilled. The reality is that schools have become too costly for this dream to come true.

author: Everett Reimer
text: No child, however, fails to learn from school. Those who never get in learn that the good things of life are not for them.
narration: Every child learns something from school, even those who never attend; they learn that the good things in life are not meant for them.

author: Everett Reimer
text: School domesticates -- socially emasculates -- both girls and boys by a process much more pervasive than mere selection by sex. School requires conformity for survival and thus shapes its students to conform to the norms for survival. If learning the official curriculum of the school were the principal criterion, this might still not be so bad, although it would substitute the learning of what Whitehead and other philosophers of education have called dead knowledge for true learning.
narration: Schools domesticate and socially emasculate both girls and boys, enforcing conformity for survival and shaping students to fit societal norms. If the official curriculum were the main focus, it might not be so detrimental, but it would replace true learning with what philosophers call dead knowledge.

author: Everett Reimer
text: The main reason we need alternatives to schools is because they close the door to humanity's escape from this monopoly.
narration: We need alternatives to schools because they shut the door on humanity's escape from this monopoly.

author: Everett Reimer
text: The school itself, as custodian of ever larger numbers of people, for increasing proportions of their life span, for an ever growing number of hours and interests, is well on the way to joining armies, prisons and insane asylums as one of society's total institutions.
narration: As the custodian of more people for longer periods, the school is becoming akin to armies, prisons, and asylums, evolving into one of society's total institutions.

author: Everett Reimer
text: Schools have grown so fast partly because they happened to be doing what was important to a technological era when this era began.
narration: Schools have rapidly expanded because they aligned with the needs of a technological era from its inception.

author: Everett Reimer
text: What schools define as merit is principally the advantage of having literate parents, books in the home, the opportunity to travel, etc.
narration: Schools define merit mainly as the advantages of having educated parents, access to books, and opportunities for travel.

author: George Dennison
text: If we go on failing much longer, stunting and wrecking as we have the lives and spirits of millions of children, it can only be because for peculiar and dreadful reasons of our own that is what we really want to do. What the book is about may be summed up in Dennison's statement, that might well be on every wall of every school of education in America, that the business of a school is not, or should not be, mere instruction, but the life of the child.
narration: If we continue to fail, stunting and wrecking the lives and spirits of millions of children, it can only be because, for peculiar and dreadful reasons, that's what we truly want to do. Dennison sums up the essence of the book with a statement that should be on every wall of every school of education in America: the purpose of a school is not mere instruction, but the life of the child.

author: George Dennison
text: the barrier of compulsion, by which he means simply that in proportion as we demand or hold over children the power to compel we give up and lose the power to influence and help.
narration: The barrier of compulsion means that the more we demand or hold power over children, the more we lose our ability to influence and help them.

author: George Dennison
text: Jose's reading problem is Jose. Or to put it another way there is no such thing as a reading problem.
narration: Jose's reading problem is simply Jose. In other words, there is no such thing as a reading problem.

author: George Dennison
text: The second has to do with the word 'belong.' Our educational system, at least at its middle- and upper middleclass layers, likes to say and indeed believes that an important part of its task is transmitting to the young the heritage of the past, the great traditions of history and culture. The effort is an unqualified failure. The proof we see all around us.
narration: The second point concerns the word 'belong.' Our educational system, especially in middle and upper-class layers, claims that a key part of its mission is to transmit the heritage of the past and the great traditions of history and culture. Yet this effort is an unqualified failure, and the proof is evident all around us.

author: George Dennison
text: The question is not really one of authority, though it is usually argued in that form. When adults give up authority, the freedom of children is not necessarily increased. Freedom is not motion in a vacuum, but motion in a continuum. If we want to know what freedom is, we must discover what the continuum is.
narration: The issue isn't solely about authority, even though that's often how it's framed. When adults relinquish authority, it doesn't automatically enhance children's freedom. Freedom isn't just movement in isolation; it's movement within a continuum. To truly understand freedom, we need to explore what that continuum entails.

author: George Dennison
text: Our concern for freedom is our concern for fulfillment - of activities we deem important and of persons we know are unique. To give freedom means to stand out of the way of the formative powers possessed by others.
narration: Our focus on freedom is really about ensuring fulfillment—fulfillment of activities we value and of individuals we recognize as unique. Granting freedom involves stepping aside and allowing others' inherent growth to flourish.

author: George Dennison
text: The business of a school is not, or should not be, mere instruction, but the life of the child. This is especially important under such conditions as we experience today.
narration: A school's purpose shouldn't just be about instruction; it should center on the child's life. This is crucial, especially given the challenging circumstances we face today.

author: George Dennison
text: If, as parents, we were to take as our concern not the instruction of our children, but the lives of our children, we would find that our schools could be used in a powerfully regenerative way.
narration: If parents shifted their focus from merely instructing their children to truly caring about their lives, we could transform our schools into powerful spaces for regeneration.

author: George Dennison
text: It can be stated axiomatically that the schoolchild's chief expense of energy is self-defense against the environment. When this culminates in impairment of growth-- and it almost always does--it is quite hopeless to reverse the trend by teaching phonics instead of Look-Say. The environment itself must be changed.
narration: It's clear that a child's main effort goes into defending themselves against their environment. When this leads to stunted growth, simply changing teaching methods won't help; the entire environment needs to be transformed.

author: George Dennison
text: We cannot raise children to be free men by treating them like little robots; we cannot produce adult democrats by putting children in lock step and placing all decisions in the hands of authorities.
narration: We can't nurture free individuals by treating children like machines or by enforcing strict obedience to authority.

author: George Dennison
text: Compulsory attendance is not merely a law which somehow enforces itself, but is ultimately an act of force.
narration: Mandatory attendance isn't just a rule; it's fundamentally an act of coercion.

author: George Dennison
text: The existence of Youth House, and of the truant officer, was of hot concern to two of our boys. They understood very well the meaning of compulsory attendance, and understanding it, they had not attended.
narration: The presence of the Youth House and truant officers deeply affected two of our boys. They grasped what compulsory attendance meant, and as a result, they chose not to attend.

author: George Dennison
text: The problem at this school was that the director himself regarded staff and students less as people than as events in his own protracted crusade against middle-class America. The faculty, too, consisted of True Believers, and I had never before seen such a listless, resentful bunch, or heard the words 'creativity' and 'spontaneity' bandied about quite so often. When the young are experiencing conflicts within themselves, it is disastrous for the teacher to 'take sides'; and no vice is more prevalent among teachers than taking sides against the parents.
narration: At this school, the director viewed staff and students more as pawns in his crusade against middle-class America than as individuals. The faculty, too, was made up of True Believers, creating a listless and resentful atmosphere, where terms like 'creativity' and 'spontaneity' were thrown around without substance. When young people are torn by internal conflicts, it's a disaster for teachers to take sides, especially against parents.

author: George Dennison
text: If compulsion is damaging and unwise, its antithesis--a vacuum of free choice--is unreal. And in fact we cannot deal with the problem in these terms, for the real question is not, What shall we do about classes? but, What shall we do about our relationships with the young?
narration: If compulsion is harmful and unwise, then the opposite—a complete lack of choice—is also unrealistic. We can't just focus on classes; the real question is how we can improve our relationships with young people.

author: George Dennison
text: The essential thing was the absence of compulsion.
narration: The key factor was the absence of compulsion.

author: George Dennison
text: School is established not in order that it should be convenient for the children to study, but that the teachers should be able to teach in comfort. The children's conversation, motion, and merriment, which are their necessary conditions of study, are not convenient for the teacher, and so in the schools—which are built on the plan of prisons—questions, conversation and motion are prohibited.
narration: Schools are set up not for the convenience of children, but for the comfort of teachers. The necessary conditions for children's study—like conversation, movement, and play—are often seen as inconvenient for teachers, leading to a school environment that resembles a prison, where questions and motion are not allowed.

author: George Dennison
text: The ethical necessity ceases to arise from the child's own experience, and now lands on his head from above, often in such grossly simplified forms that it can no longer be called ethical.
narration: The ethical demands on children shift from their own experiences to imposed rules from above, often simplified to the point where they lose their ethical meaning.

author: Herbert Kohl
text: Fear is only overcome through risk and experimentation. As I became familiar with the children in 6-1 I became more willing to respond to the children individually and less dependent on the protection of the role of teacher. I let an insult pass and discovered that the rest of the class didn’t take up the insult; I learned to say nothing when Ralph returned from pacing the halls or when Alvin refused to do arithmetic.
narration: Overcoming fear requires taking risks and experimenting. As I got to know the children in 6-1, I became more open to responding to them as individuals, rather than relying solely on my authority as a teacher. I learned to let insults slide and found that the rest of the class didn't engage with them; I also learned to remain silent when Ralph returned from pacing the halls or when Alvin refused to do arithmetic.

author: Herbert Kohl
text: An atmosphere must exist in the classroom where conflict, disagreement and irrationality are accepted temporary occurrences. No child, because he defies, should thereby have to become ‘a defiant child’, or because he refuses to work, ‘a lazy child’. Such labelling makes the classroom a harsh, un-forgiving place, a world not fit for children or adults.
narration: Classrooms need to foster an environment where conflict, disagreement, and irrationality are seen as temporary occurrences. No child should be labeled as 'defiant' for standing up against authority or 'lazy' for refusing to work. Such labels create a harsh and unforgiving atmosphere, unfit for both children and adults.

author: Herbert Kohl
text: The idea that words were complex phenomena with long and compelling histories was never presented to the children. I doubt many teachers entertained it. The canons of the schools pretend that a small pre-selected segment of the language of the moment is an eternally correct and all-inclusive form.
narration: Children were never introduced to the idea that words have complex histories and meanings. I doubt many teachers even considered this. Schools often present a narrow, pre-selected segment of language as the only correct form, ignoring the richness and evolution of language.

author: Herbert Kohl
text: There were compulsory achievement and, at that time, IQ tests given halfway through the year, and it was on the results of those tests that the children’s placement in junior high school would be based. Nothing else really counted; classes were formed on the basis of reading grades and my pupils had to do well. It was a matter of their whole future since in junior high school all but those few students put in the ‘top’ classes were considered ‘not college material’ and treated with the scorn that they merited in their teachers’ eyes.
narration: There were compulsory achievement and IQ tests given halfway through the year, and the results determined the children's placement in junior high school. Nothing else mattered; classes were formed based on reading grades, and my students had to perform well. Their entire future hinged on this, as only a few would be placed in the 'top' classes, while the rest were deemed 'not college material' and treated with disdain by their teachers.

author: Herbert Kohl
text: The children were angry and shocked; no one had ever come right out and told them they were failing. It was always put so nicely and evasively that the children never knew where they stood. After seeing the IQ scores — only two of which were above 100, the majority being in the 80 to 90 range — and the reading scores, which with few exceptions were below grade level, the children were furious.
narration: The children were furious and shocked; no one had ever been straightforward about their failures. It was always presented in a nice, evasive manner, leaving them unaware of their actual standing. After seeing their IQ scores — with only two above 100 and most in the 80 to 90 range — and reading scores that were generally below grade level, their anger was palpable.

author: Herbert Kohl
text: The children learned that they could do unpleasant but necessary work; they also knew that the test preparation was not all there was to education, that the substance of their work, the novels and stories, the poems and projects they created, were the essential thing no matter how the external world chose to judge them.
narration: The children discovered they could tackle unpleasant but necessary tasks. They understood that test preparation wasn't the entirety of education; the essence lay in their work—novels, stories, poems, and projects—regardless of how the outside world judged them.

author: Herbert Kohl
text: They knew they were rejects in the school, and also knew that the school as a whole was a reject.
narration: They recognized they were seen as rejects by the school, which itself felt like a reject.

author: Herbert Kohl
text: The most frequent epithet they used in describing the children was ‘animals’.
narration: Teachers often referred to the children as 'animals'.

author: Herbert Kohl
text: They can talk to me of their failure and despair — unfortunately I understand it.
narration: They confided in me about their failures and despair, and sadly, I understood their struggles.

author: Herbert Kohl
text: Grace has become alienated from Harlem, her home, her friends, her very self, in attempting to be part of the prep-school world. But she can’t fully participate there; she’s too poor, too lonely, too much of a special case. It is hard to know what will come of her alienation, whether it will pull her apart or whether she will transcend it and show us adults a way to synthesize the contradictions we make and allow in our society.
narration: Grace feels alienated from her home and friends in Harlem as she tries to fit into the prep-school world. Yet, she struggles to fully belong there, feeling too poor and lonely. It's uncertain what her alienation will lead to—whether it will tear her apart or if she'll find a way to reconcile the contradictions in her life.

author: Herbert Kohl
text: The thirty-six children are suffering from the diseases of our society. They are no special cases; there are too many hundreds of thousands like them, lost in indifferent, inferior schools, put on the streets or in prep schools with condescension or cynicism.
narration: These thirty-six children reflect a broader issue in society, suffering from systemic problems. They are not unique; countless others are lost in inadequate schools, facing indifference and cynicism.

author: Paul Goodman
text: The mass superstition in question, which is the target of this classic and iconoclastic work, is that education can only be achieved by the use of institutions like the school. Paul Goodman argues that on the contrary subjecting young people to institutionalized learning stunts and distorts their natural intellectual development makes them hostile to the very idea of education and finally turns out regimented competitive citizens likely only to aggravate our current social ills.
narration: Goodman challenges the belief that education can only happen within schools, arguing that institutionalized learning actually hinders natural intellectual growth and creates competitive citizens who exacerbate social issues.

author: Paul Goodman
text: What prevents it is almost demonstrable that, for many children, it is precisely going to school that prevents -- because of the school’s alien style, banning of spontaneous interest, extrinsic rewards and punishments.
narration: Goodman points out that for many children, school itself is the barrier to learning, stifling their natural curiosity and interest.

author: Paul Goodman
text: It is a very grave mistake to think that the enjoyment of seeing and searching can be promoted by means of coercion and a sense of duty.
narration: Goodman reflects on the misconception that coercion can foster genuine curiosity and enjoyment in learning.

author: Paul Goodman
text: The public schools of America have indeed been a powerful, and beneficent force for the democratizing of a great mixed population. But we must be careful to keep reassessing them when, with changing conditions, they become a universal trap and democracy begins to look like regimentation.
narration: While public schools have played a crucial role in democratizing society, Goodman warns that we must continuously evaluate their effectiveness to prevent them from becoming a trap that leads to regimentation.

author: Paul Goodman
text: The schools less and less represent any human values, but simply adjustment to a mechanical system. The implication is that the 'middle class', for all its virtues, is obsessional, prejudiced, and prudish. A teacher must try to teach each child in terms of what he brings, his background, his habits, the language he understands. But if taken to be more than technical, it is a disastrous conception.
narration: Schools increasingly fail to embody human values, merely adjusting students to a mechanical system. This suggests that the middle class, despite its virtues, can be obsessional and prejudiced. Teachers should tailor their approach to each child's unique background and habits, but when this is viewed as merely technical, it becomes a harmful misunderstanding.

author: Paul Goodman
text: The reasonable social policy would be not to have these youths in school, certainly not in high school, but to educate them otherwise and provide opportunity for a decent future in some other way.
narration: A sensible approach would be to remove these youths from school, especially high school, and find alternative ways to educate them and offer better opportunities.

author: Paul Goodman
text: The compulsory system has become a universal trap, and it is no good.
narration: The compulsory education system has turned into a universal trap that fails to serve its purpose.

author: Paul Goodman
text: The young rightly resist animal constraint. But, at least in New York where I have been a school-board visitor, most teachers -- and the principals who supervise their classes -- operate as if progressive education had not proved the case for noise and freedom of bodily motion.
narration: Young people naturally resist constraints. However, in New York schools, many teachers and principals seem unaware of the benefits of progressive education, which advocates for noise and freedom of movement.

author: Paul Goodman
text: Why, I suppose it's to undo the damage done at home, so the child can begin to breathe again and be curious. At this the student-teachers laughed and asked what was the purpose of high school. I had to answer of course, 'To undo the damage done by the elementary schools.'
narration: I suppose the main purpose of elementary schools is to help children recover from the damage done at home, allowing them to breathe and be curious. When asked about high school, I had to admit that its purpose seems to be to undo the damage caused by elementary schools.

author: Paul Goodman
text: The advance-guard problem is that the compulsory school system, like the whole of our economy, politics and standard of living, has become a lockstep. It is no longer designed for the maximum growth and future practical utility of the children into a changing world, but is inept social engineering for extrinsic goals, pitifully short-range.
narration: The compulsory school system has become a rigid lockstep, no longer focused on fostering children's growth and adapting to a changing world. Instead, it's a form of social engineering aimed at short-term, external goals.

author: Paul Goodman
text: Twist it and turn it how you will, there is no logic to the proposal to extend compulsory schooling except as a device to keep the unemployed off the streets by putting them into concentration camps called schools. The Continuation branch of Milwaukee Vocational is, then, typical of what we can expect. As an academic, I am appalled by this motivation for schooling. As a citizen and human being, this waste of youthful vitality appalls me.
narration: No matter how you look at it, extending compulsory schooling seems to serve only to keep the unemployed off the streets, essentially turning schools into concentration camps. The Milwaukee Vocational school exemplifies this troubling trend. As an academic, I find this motivation for schooling deeply troubling, and as a citizen, I am horrified by the waste of youthful potential.

author: Paul Goodman
text: The overcrowding precludes individuality or spontaneity, reduces the young to ciphers, and the teacher to a martinet. If a youth tries to follow his own bent, he is interrupted and even jailed. If he does not perform, he is humiliated and threatened, but he is not allowed to fail and get out.
narration: Overcrowding in schools stifles individuality and spontaneity, turning students into mere numbers and teachers into enforcers. If a student attempts to pursue their own interests, they face interruptions and even punishment. Failure is not an option, leading to humiliation and threats instead of the freedom to learn.

author: Paul Goodman
text: Education must foster independent thought and expression, rather than conformity.
narration: Education should encourage independent thought and creativity, not conformity.

author: Paul Goodman
text: A child is confused. And he is also anxious, because if the information is not correctly parroted, he will fall off the school ladder and be a drop-out; or he will not be hep among his little friends. At a childish level, all this adds up to brainwashing. The components are (a) a uniform world-view, (b) the absence of any viable alternative, (c) confusion about the relevance of one's own experience and feelings, and (d) a chronic anxiety, so that one clings to the one world-view as the only security.
narration: Children often feel confused and anxious in school, fearing that if they can't memorize information correctly, they'll fall behind or be excluded from their peers. This pressure creates a kind of brainwashing, where they adopt a uniform worldview without viable alternatives, leading to confusion about their own experiences and feelings.

author: Paul Goodman
text: The social machine does not require or desire its youth to find identity or vocation; it is interested only in aptitude. It does not want new initiative, but conformity.
narration: The system doesn't encourage young people to discover their identities or pursue their passions; instead, it prioritizes conformity and mere aptitude.

author: Paul Goodman
text: The adolescent sub-culture that persists is irrelevant to the business going on, except to sabotage it, but the adolescent community is not replaced by close acquaintance with learned adults. The teachers hold the students off and... it is a chief function of orderly administration to keep the students out of contact with the teachers and the teachers out of contact with one another.
narration: The ongoing adolescent subculture often feels disconnected from the educational process, as the system keeps students and teachers apart, hindering meaningful interactions.

author: Paul Goodman
text: The dialogue with the subject matter, with Nature and History, is as skimpy as with the teacher. Colleges are not interested in such things any more -- it has little Ph.D. value. The student is told the current doctrine and is trained to give it back accurately.
narration: In colleges, the focus has shifted away from meaningful dialogue about subjects like Nature and History. Instead, students are simply taught to regurgitate current doctrines without engaging deeply with the material.

author: Paul Goodman
text: The quality or meaning of it makes little difference; but a society is in a desperate way when its music makes little difference. In fact, of course, these frivolous things are where normally a child would explore his feelings and find his identity and vocation, learn to be responsible; nevertheless, if any of them threatens to interfere with the serious business -- a hobby that interferes with homework, or dating that makes a youth want to quit school and get a job, it is unhesitatingly interrupted, sometimes with threats and sanctions.
narration: The quality or meaning of these activities seems insignificant, yet it's alarming when a society's music holds no real value. These seemingly trivial pursuits are where children typically explore their feelings, discover their identities, and learn responsibility. However, if any of these activities threaten to disrupt the serious business of schoolwork or relationships, they are swiftly curtailed, often with threats.

author: Paul Goodman
text: The harsh facts and the relativity of morals are bound to be embarrassing and shocking. Regarded as 'assignments' -- as high-school graduates must regard them -- the voluminous readings are indigestible straw and are annotated by lore; more mature students might be able to take them as books.
narration: The harsh realities and moral complexities can be quite shocking. When viewed merely as assignments, the extensive readings become overwhelming and superficial; only more mature students might be able to appreciate them as genuine literature.

author: Paul Goodman
text: The crucial issue is the change from the humanism of independent guilds of scholars, whether in the liberal arts or the professions, to a system of social engineering for the national economy and polity.
narration: The key issue is the shift from humanistic scholarship to a system focused on social engineering for economic and political ends.

author: Paul Goodman
text: The hard task of education is to liberate and strengthen a youth's initiative, and at the same time to see to it that he knows what is necessary to cope with the ongoing activities and culture of society, so that his initiative can be relevant. It is absurd to think that this task can be accomplished by so much sitting in a box facing front, manipulating symbols at the direction of distant administrators. This is rather a way to regiment and brainwash.
narration: Education should focus on liberating a youth's initiative and preparing them for the real world, rather than confining them to a classroom where they merely follow orders. This approach risks turning education into a means of control.

author: Paul Goodman
text: It suits him so badly. He is bright but not bookish, curious but not scholarly, teachable but not in this way. He must be educated; everybody must be educated; but this kind of schooling has certainly not been the best way to educate him.
narration: This young man is not thriving in the current educational system. He is intelligent and curious, yet the traditional schooling methods are failing to engage him effectively.

author: Paul Goodman
text: The unfortunate part of the expansion was that, insensibly, everybody began to believe that being in school was the only way to become an educated person. What a generation before had been the usual course, to quit school and seek elsewhere to grow up, became a sign of eccentricity, failure, delinquency. Suddenly, since the Korean War and hysterically since Sputnik there has developed a disastrous overestimation of schools and scholarship.
narration: It's unfortunate that over time, society has come to believe that being in school is the only path to education. What was once seen as a normal choice to leave school and grow up has now become viewed as eccentric or a failure. Since events like the Korean War and the launch of Sputnik, we've seen a troubling overestimation of the value of schools and scholarship.

author: Paul Goodman
text: The schools both relieve the home and rescue the children from the home. But the criteria for baby-sitting are to be safe and enjoyable; our primary schools are safe enough but not enjoyable. As a means of socialization and democratization the primary schools have gone much too far towards regimentation.
narration: Schools serve to relieve the home environment and provide safety for children, but they often lack enjoyment. In their role of socializing and democratizing, primary schools have become overly regimented.

author: Paul Goodman
text: Elementary schooling illustrates at its worst the human propensity to impose an unnecessary system and make it hard for ourselves, and thereby to lose the goods that are easy.

author: Paul Goodman
text: There is no evidence that highly creative youngsters in the sciences, arts or professions especially thrive on formal schooling at all, rather than by exploring and gradually gravitating to the right work and environment.

author: Peter Gray
text: Evaluation drives a wedge between those who already know how and those who don’t, pushing the former up and the latter down. Evaluation has this pernicious effect because it produces a mind-set that is opposite from the playful state of mind, which is the ideal state for learning new skills, solving new problems, and engaging in all sorts of creative activities.
narration: Evaluation creates a divide between those who already know how to succeed and those who struggle, elevating the former while pushing the latter down. This harmful effect arises because evaluation fosters a mindset that contradicts the playful state of mind, which is essential for learning new skills, solving problems, and engaging in creative activities.

author: Peter Gray

narration: At its core, play is an expression of freedom.

author: John Taylor Gatto

text: Our form of schooling has turned us into dependent, emotionally needy, excessively childish people who wait for a teacher to tell them what to do.

author: John Taylor Gatto

text: The industrial model, with pyramidal management and plenty of horizontal featherbedding niches, is based on ignorance of how things get done, or indifference to results.

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: The men who followed Washington lived, too, because the leader they chose was not a function of some greater abstraction.

author: John Taylor Gatto

author: John Taylor Gatto
source: The Underground History of American Education
text: If they mean to have a war, let it begin now.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Our schools leave no time for kids to be with parents and the community.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Learning can’t take place in pieces of time cut out for the convenience of an institution or in lessons set apart from the world in which students live.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: We have to start from the ground up and reconsider what education is.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: The sorry level of discourse in America should tell us that the imaginations of our citizens are being cheated by the desperate ineffectiveness of schools.

text: Words and meaningless routines insulate people from life itself, blind them to what is happening around them, and deaden the moral faculties.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: The method is deeply and profoundly anti-educational. No tinkering will fix it.

author: John Taylor Gatto

text: It’s a national disease, this dependency and aimlessness, and I think schooling and television and lessons have a lot to do with it.

author: John Taylor Gatto

author: John Taylor Gatto

text: A person imprisoned by these schemes is like a plant in a flowerpot too small for it. He cannot grow or mature.

author: John Taylor Gatto

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: It’s time to stop. This system doesn’t work, and it’s one of the causes of our world coming apart.

author: John Taylor Gatto

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: What on earth is going on? Any genuine debate would have to grapple with the uniform failure of every type of government monopoly school.

author: John Taylor Gatto

text: It is a lie because the changing dynamics of time and situation and locality render expertise irrelevant and obsolete shortly after it is anointed.

author: John Taylor Gatto

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: He questioned the validity and relevance of standardized tests, the prison atmosphere of school, and the lack of relevant experience given students.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Unfortunately, our school did not allow that dialogue to happen, except for a few teachers who had the courage to engage the students.

author: John Taylor Gatto

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: It is a measure of the power of Gatto’s ideas, their urgency, and their continuing relevance that school authorities are still trying to shut them out 12 years after their initial publication, afraid even to debate them.
narration: Demonstrates the ongoing struggle against oppressive educational systems.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: I taught for thirty years in some of the worst schools in Manhattan, and in some of the best, and during that time I became an expert in boredom.

author: John Taylor Gatto

narration: Describes the negative atmosphere among teachers, reflecting on the systemic issues in schools.

author: John Taylor Gatto

author: John Taylor Gatto

author: John Taylor Gatto

author: John Taylor Gatto

author: John Taylor Gatto

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: It was from James Bryant Conant... that I first got wind of the real purposes of American schooling.
narration: Introduces influential figures who shaped the educational system's goals.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: School is meant to determine each student’s proper social role.

author: John Taylor Gatto

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: Our schools are. . . factories in which the raw products (children) are to be shaped and fashioned.

author: John Taylor Gatto
text: School trains children to be employees and consumers; teach your own to be leaders and adventurers.
